revision.comment: Text replacement - "粘土槨}}" to "粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}}"
revision.text:
[[File:Kuroduka kofun museum02s3872.jpg|200px|right|thumb|{{ill|Cinnabar|ja|辰砂}}-coated {{ill|Waritakegata wooden coffin|ja|割竹形木棺}} replica inside the stone chamber of the {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}}. Visible are the base of the coffin and the {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirror|ja|三角縁神獣鏡}}.]] '''Waritakegata Wooden Coffin''' (割竹形木棺, *Waritakegata Mokukan*) refers to a type of hollowed-out wooden coffin commonly found during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. == Overview == This type of coffin is made by splitting a log lengthwise, hollowing out its interior to create the coffin body and lid, and forming an elongated cylindrical shape. It was often placed inside a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}}.
The waritakegata wooden coffin is believed to have been conceived as a solemn burial vessel for the chieftain class and privileged elites buried in ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}}.
In well-preserved examples, the interior is divided into three sections: the deceased is placed in the central section, with grave goods arranged in the head and foot sections. The coffins were often placed on clay beds, gravel layers, or in chambers equipped with drainage systems, indicating meticulous construction of the burial facilities. Red pigments such as {{ill|Bengala|lt=red ochre|ja|ベンガラ}} (iron(III) oxide, Fe₂O₃) or cinnabar (mercury(II) sulfide, HgS) were sometimes applied to the interior and exterior of the coffin or the walls of the stone chamber. These acts are considered to have had ritual significance.Yoshitaka Hōjō, "Pit-style stone chambers and burial rituals," in {{ill|Makoto Sahara|ja|佐原真}}, 2007, pp. 451–453. == Construction and Materials == The coffins are made by hollowing out giant trees 5 to 8 meters in length and approximately 1 meter in diameter, resulting in the "waritakegata" (split-bamboo) shape. Both ends of the cylindrical coffin were sealed with wooden boards.
In addition to the waritakegata type, there were {{ill|boat-shaped wooden coffins|ja|舟形木棺}}, named for their resemblance to boat prows. These are less commonly found. Most of the source wood for the waritakegata coffins, especially west of the {{ill|Kinki region|ja|近畿地方}}, was evergreen conifers such as {{ill|Sciadopitys verticillata|lt=Kōyamaki|ja|コウヤマキ}}, whereas regions east of {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}} used {{ill|cedar|ja|スギ}}, {{ill|zelkova|ja|ケヤキ}}, and {{ill|hinoki cypress|ja|ヒノキ}}.The {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} contained a coffin made from {{ill|mulberry|ja|クワ}} wood, suggesting the interred may have been connected to sericulture or possibly a female ruler.Examples include cedar from the Ichimiyake Higashi Site (Shiga Prefecture), hinoki from Hitachi Kagami Tumulus (Ibaraki Prefecture), and zelkova from Eimoridzuka Tumulus 2 (Yamagata Prefecture). Few coffins survive intact due to decay, and many structural details remain unknown.The shape of the coffin's bottom is often preserved as an arc-shaped trace on the coffin bed, even when the coffin itself has decayed. == Timeframe and Scale == Waritakegata coffins appeared in the early Kofun period, sometimes even in late {{ill|Yayoi tumulus burials|ja|弥生墳丘墓}}. While originally placed inside pit-style stone chambers or stone cists, later periods saw coffins encased in clay or gravel ({{ill|clay coffins|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}}, gravel cists) or buried directly into grave pits. {{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄}} established the connection between waritakegata coffins and burial facilities, and they are considered a defining feature of standardized early kofun burials.Morishta (2005), pp. 55–56. {{ill|Yoshirō Kondō|ja|近藤義郎}} proposed that {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} served as ritual sites for the succession of chieftain spirits, highlighting: # A significant leap in mound shape and scale. # The presence of waritakegata coffins and pit-style stone chambers. # Grave goods, including numerous {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡}}, following specific norms.Kondō (1986). {{ill|Kazuo Hirose|ja|広瀬和雄}} emphasized the unique features of early kofun, noting, for instance, the pairing of waritakegata coffins with pit-style stone cists in the {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} and {{ill|Moto Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳}}, compared to {{ill|Akatuka Kofun|ja|赤塚古墳}} in Oita, which used box-shaped stone coffins.Hirose (2003), pp. 155–156. The size of the burial chamber often correlated with the size of the coffin.Ōtsuka and Kobayashi, "Kofun Dictionary," 1982, pp. 367–368. Early examples measured 5–8 meters in length but became shorter over time, with late-period coffins averaging 2–3 meters. By the late Kofun period, boat-shaped coffins and large stone coffins gave way to box-shaped wooden coffins. == Notable Tombs and Burial Mounds == '''Yayoi period''' * {{ill|Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja|市三宅東遺跡}} (Late Yayoi, Shiga Prefecture) * {{ill|Hirabaru Burial Site|ja|平原遺跡}} (Late Yayoi, Fukuoka Prefecture) '''Kofun period''' * {{ill|Eimoridzuka Tumulus 2|ja|衛守塚古墳群}} (Early, Yamagata Prefecture) * {{ill|Tōmimizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳}} (Late Early, Miyagi Prefecture) * {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} (Early, Nara Prefecture) == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Kofun Dictionary|last=Ōtsuka|coauthors=Kobayashi|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|year=1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|title=The Birth of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Kondō|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|year=1986|isbn=4-00-010266-4}} * {{Cite book|title=The State of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Hirose|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=2003|isbn=4-04-703355-3}} == External Links == * revision.comment: Text replacement - "三角縁神獣鏡}}" to "三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}}"
revision.text:
[[File:Kuroduka kofun museum02s3872.jpg|200px|right|thumb|{{ill|Cinnabar|ja|辰砂}}-coated {{ill|Waritakegata wooden coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan}} replica inside the stone chamber of the {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}}. Visible are the base of the coffin and the {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirror|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}}.]] '''Waritakegata Wooden Coffin''' (割竹形木棺, *Waritakegata Mokukan*) refers to a type of hollowed-out wooden coffin commonly found during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. == Overview == This type of coffin is made by splitting a log lengthwise, hollowing out its interior to create the coffin body and lid, and forming an elongated cylindrical shape. It was often placed inside a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}}.
The waritakegata wooden coffin is believed to have been conceived as a solemn burial vessel for the chieftain class and privileged elites buried in ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}}.
In well-preserved examples, the interior is divided into three sections: the deceased is placed in the central section, with grave goods arranged in the head and foot sections. The coffins were often placed on clay beds, gravel layers, or in chambers equipped with drainage systems, indicating meticulous construction of the burial facilities. Red pigments such as {{ill|Bengala|lt=red ochre|ja|ベンガラ}} (iron(III) oxide, Fe₂O₃) or cinnabar (mercury(II) sulfide, HgS) were sometimes applied to the interior and exterior of the coffin or the walls of the stone chamber. These acts are considered to have had ritual significance.Yoshitaka Hōjō, "Pit-style stone chambers and burial rituals," in {{ill|Makoto Sahara|ja|佐原真}}, 2007, pp. 451–453. == Construction and Materials == The coffins are made by hollowing out giant trees 5 to 8 meters in length and approximately 1 meter in diameter, resulting in the "waritakegata" (split-bamboo) shape. Both ends of the cylindrical coffin were sealed with wooden boards.
In addition to the waritakegata type, there were {{ill|boat-shaped wooden coffins|ja|舟形木棺}}, named for their resemblance to boat prows. These are less commonly found. Most of the source wood for the waritakegata coffins, especially west of the {{ill|Kinki region|ja|近畿地方}}, was evergreen conifers such as {{ill|Sciadopitys verticillata|lt=Kōyamaki|ja|コウヤマキ}}, whereas regions east of {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}} used {{ill|cedar|ja|スギ}}, {{ill|zelkova|ja|ケヤキ}}, and {{ill|hinoki cypress|ja|ヒノキ}}.The {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} contained a coffin made from {{ill|mulberry|ja|クワ}} wood, suggesting the interred may have been connected to sericulture or possibly a female ruler.Examples include cedar from the Ichimiyake Higashi Site (Shiga Prefecture), hinoki from Hitachi Kagami Tumulus (Ibaraki Prefecture), and zelkova from Eimoridzuka Tumulus 2 (Yamagata Prefecture). Few coffins survive intact due to decay, and many structural details remain unknown.The shape of the coffin's bottom is often preserved as an arc-shaped trace on the coffin bed, even when the coffin itself has decayed. == Timeframe and Scale == Waritakegata coffins appeared in the early Kofun period, sometimes even in late {{ill|Yayoi tumulus burials|ja|弥生墳丘墓}}. While originally placed inside pit-style stone chambers or stone cists, later periods saw coffins encased in clay or gravel ({{ill|clay coffins|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}}, gravel cists) or buried directly into grave pits. {{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄}} established the connection between waritakegata coffins and burial facilities, and they are considered a defining feature of standardized early kofun burials.Morishta (2005), pp. 55–56. {{ill|Yoshirō Kondō|ja|近藤義郎}} proposed that {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} served as ritual sites for the succession of chieftain spirits, highlighting: # A significant leap in mound shape and scale. # The presence of waritakegata coffins and pit-style stone chambers. # Grave goods, including numerous {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}}, following specific norms.Kondō (1986). {{ill|Kazuo Hirose|ja|広瀬和雄}} emphasized the unique features of early kofun, noting, for instance, the pairing of waritakegata coffins with pit-style stone cists in the {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} and {{ill|Moto Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳}}, compared to {{ill|Akatuka Kofun|ja|赤塚古墳}} in Oita, which used box-shaped stone coffins.Hirose (2003), pp. 155–156. The size of the burial chamber often correlated with the size of the coffin.Ōtsuka and Kobayashi, "Kofun Dictionary," 1982, pp. 367–368. Early examples measured 5–8 meters in length but became shorter over time, with late-period coffins averaging 2–3 meters. By the late Kofun period, boat-shaped coffins and large stone coffins gave way to box-shaped wooden coffins. == Notable Tombs and Burial Mounds == '''Yayoi period''' * {{ill|Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja|市三宅東遺跡}} (Late Yayoi, Shiga Prefecture) * {{ill|Hirabaru Burial Site|ja|平原遺跡}} (Late Yayoi, Fukuoka Prefecture) '''Kofun period''' * {{ill|Eimoridzuka Tumulus 2|ja|衛守塚古墳群}} (Early, Yamagata Prefecture) * {{ill|Tōmimizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳}} (Late Early, Miyagi Prefecture) * {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} (Early, Nara Prefecture) == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Kofun Dictionary|last=Ōtsuka|coauthors=Kobayashi|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|year=1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|title=The Birth of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Kondō|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|year=1986|isbn=4-00-010266-4}} * {{Cite book|title=The State of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Hirose|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=2003|isbn=4-04-703355-3}} == External Links == * By the late Kofun period, boat-shaped coffins and large stone coffins gave way to box-shaped wooden coffins. == Notable Tombs and Burial Mounds == '''Yayoi period''' * {{ill|Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja|市三宅東遺跡}} (Late Yayoi, Shiga Prefecture) * {{ill|Hirabaru Burial Site|ja|平原遺跡}} (Late Yayoi, Fukuoka Prefecture) '''Kofun period''' * {{ill|Eimoridzuka Tumulus 2|ja|衛守塚古墳群}} (Early, Yamagata Prefecture) * {{ill|Tōmimizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳}} (Late Early, Miyagi Prefecture) * {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} (Early, Nara Prefecture) == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Kofun Mokkan]][[Category:前方後円墳]] [[Category:前方後円墳]][[sn:Waritakegata Mokkan]][[Category:前方後円墳]] [[ja:割竹形木棺]]

[[File:Kuroduka kofun museum02s3872.jpg|200px|right|thumb|{{ill|Cinnabar|ja|辰砂}}-coated {{ill|Waritakegata wooden coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan}} replica inside the stone chamber of the {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}}. Visible are the base of the coffin and the {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirror|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}}.]] '''Waritakegata Wooden Coffin''' (割竹形木棺, *Waritakegata Mokukan*) refers to a type of hollowed-out wooden coffin commonly found during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. == Overview == This type of coffin is made by splitting a log lengthwise, hollowing out its interior to create the coffin body and lid, and forming an elongated cylindrical shape. It was often placed inside a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}}.
The waritakegata wooden coffin is believed to have been conceived as a solemn burial vessel for the chieftain class and privileged elites buried in ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}}.
In well-preserved examples, the interior is divided into three sections: the deceased is placed in the central section, with grave goods arranged in the head and foot sections. The coffins were often placed on clay beds, gravel layers, or in chambers equipped with drainage systems, indicating meticulous construction of the burial facilities. Red pigments such as {{ill|Bengala|lt=red ochre|ja|ベンガラ}} (iron(III) oxide, Fe₂O₃) or cinnabar (mercury(II) sulfide, HgS) were sometimes applied to the interior and exterior of the coffin or the walls of the stone chamber. These acts are considered to have had ritual significance.Yoshitaka Hōjō, "Pit-style stone chambers and burial rituals," in {{ill|Makoto Sahara|ja|佐原真}}, 2007, pp. 451–453. == Construction and Materials == The coffins are made by hollowing out giant trees 5 to 8 meters in length and approximately 1 meter in diameter, resulting in the "waritakegata" (split-bamboo) shape. Both ends of the cylindrical coffin were sealed with wooden boards.
In addition to the waritakegata type, there were {{ill|boat-shaped wooden coffins|ja|舟形木棺}}, named for their resemblance to boat prows. These are less commonly found. Most of the source wood for the waritakegata coffins, especially west of the {{ill|Kinki region|ja|近畿地方}}, was evergreen conifers such as {{ill|Sciadopitys verticillata|lt=Kōyamaki|ja|コウヤマキ}}, whereas regions east of {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}} used {{ill|cedar|ja|スギ}}, {{ill|zelkova|ja|ケヤキ}}, and {{ill|hinoki cypress|ja|ヒノキ}}.The {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} contained a coffin made from {{ill|mulberry|ja|クワ}} wood, suggesting the interred may have been connected to sericulture or possibly a female ruler.Examples include cedar from the Ichimiyake Higashi Site (Shiga Prefecture), hinoki from Hitachi Kagami Tumulus (Ibaraki Prefecture), and zelkova from Eimoridzuka Tumulus 2 (Yamagata Prefecture). Few coffins survive intact due to decay, and many structural details remain unknown.The shape of the coffin's bottom is often preserved as an arc-shaped trace on the coffin bed, even when the coffin itself has decayed. == Timeframe and Scale == Waritakegata coffins appeared in the early Kofun period, sometimes even in late {{ill|Yayoi tumulus burials|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs}}. While originally placed inside pit-style stone chambers or stone cists, later periods saw coffins encased in clay or gravel ({{ill|clay coffins|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}}, gravel cists) or buried directly into grave pits. {{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄}} established the connection between waritakegata coffins and burial facilities, and they are considered a defining feature of standardized early kofun burials.Morishta (2005), pp. 55–56. {{ill|Yoshirō Kondō|ja|近藤義郎}} proposed that {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} served as ritual sites for the succession of chieftain spirits, highlighting: # A significant leap in mound shape and scale. # The presence of waritakegata coffins and pit-style stone chambers. # Grave goods, including numerous {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}}, following specific norms.Kondō (1986). {{ill|Kazuo Hirose|ja|広瀬和雄}} emphasized the unique features of early kofun, noting, for instance, the pairing of waritakegata coffins with pit-style stone cists in the {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} and {{ill|Moto Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳}}, compared to {{ill|Akatuka Kofun|ja|赤塚古墳}} in Oita, which used box-shaped stone coffins.Hirose (2003), pp. 155–156. The size of the burial chamber often correlated with the size of the coffin.Ōtsuka and Kobayashi, "Kofun Dictionary," 1982, pp. 367–368. Early examples measured 5–8 meters in length but became shorter over time, with late-period coffins averaging 2–3 meters. By the late Kofun period, boat-shaped coffins and large stone coffins gave way to box-shaped wooden coffins. == Notable Tombs and Burial Mounds == '''Yayoi period''' * {{ill|Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja|市三宅東遺跡}} (Late Yayoi, Shiga Prefecture) * {{ill|Hirabaru Burial Site|ja|平原遺跡}} (Late Yayoi, Fukuoka Prefecture) '''Kofun period''' * {{ill|Eimoridzuka Tumulus 2|ja|衛守塚古墳群}} (Early, Yamagata Prefecture) * {{ill|Tōmimizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳}} (Late Early, Miyagi Prefecture) * {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} (Early, Nara Prefecture) == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Kofun Dictionary|last=Ōtsuka|coauthors=Kobayashi|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|year=1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|title=The Birth of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Kondō|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|year=1986|isbn=4-00-010266-4}} * {{Cite book|title=The State of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Hirose|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=2003|isbn=4-04-703355-3}} == External Links == * [[ja:割竹形木棺]]

[[File:Kuroduka kofun museum02s3872.jpg|200px|right|thumb|{{ill|Cinnabar|ja|辰砂}}-coated {{ill|Waritakegata wooden coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan}} replica inside the stone chamber of the {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}}. Visible are the base of the coffin and the {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirror|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}}.]] '''Waritakegata Wooden Coffin''' (割竹形木棺, *Waritakegata Mokukan*) refers to a type of hollowed-out wooden coffin commonly found during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. == Overview == This type of coffin is made by splitting a log lengthwise, hollowing out its interior to create the coffin body and lid, and forming an elongated cylindrical shape. It was often placed inside a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}}.
The waritakegata wooden coffin is believed to have been conceived as a solemn burial vessel for the chieftain class and privileged elites buried in ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}}.
In well-preserved examples, the interior is divided into three sections: the deceased is placed in the central section, with grave goods arranged in the head and foot sections. The coffins were often placed on clay beds, gravel layers, or in chambers equipped with drainage systems, indicating meticulous construction of the burial facilities. Red pigments such as {{ill|Bengala|lt=red ochre|ja|ベンガラ}} (iron(III) oxide, Fe₂O₃) or cinnabar (mercury(II) sulfide, HgS) were sometimes applied to the interior and exterior of the coffin or the walls of the stone chamber. These acts are considered to have had ritual significance.Yoshitaka Hōjō, "Pit-style stone chambers and burial rituals," in {{ill|Makoto Sahara|ja|佐原真}}, 2007, pp. 451–453. == Construction and Materials == The coffins are made by hollowing out giant trees 5 to 8 meters in length and approximately 1 meter in diameter, resulting in the "waritakegata" (split-bamboo) shape. Both ends of the cylindrical coffin were sealed with wooden boards.
In addition to the waritakegata type, there were {{ill|boat-shaped wooden coffins|ja|舟形木棺}}, named for their resemblance to boat prows. These are less commonly found. Most of the source wood for the waritakegata coffins, especially west of the {{ill|Kinki region|ja|近畿地方}}, was evergreen conifers such as {{ill|Sciadopitys verticillata|lt=Kōyamaki|ja|コウヤマキ}}, whereas regions east of {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}} used {{ill|cedar|ja|スギ}}, {{ill|zelkova|ja|ケヤキ}}, and {{ill|hinoki cypress|ja|ヒノキ}}.The {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} contained a coffin made from {{ill|mulberry|ja|クワ}} wood, suggesting the interred may have been connected to sericulture or possibly a female ruler.Examples include cedar from the Ichimiyake Higashi Site (Shiga Prefecture), hinoki from Hitachi Kagami Tumulus (Ibaraki Prefecture), and zelkova from Eimoridzuka Tumulus 2 (Yamagata Prefecture). Few coffins survive intact due to decay, and many structural details remain unknown.The shape of the coffin's bottom is often preserved as an arc-shaped trace on the coffin bed, even when the coffin itself has decayed. == Timeframe and Scale == Waritakegata coffins appeared in the early Kofun period, sometimes even in late {{ill|Yayoi tumulus burials|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs}}. While originally placed inside pit-style stone chambers or stone cists, later periods saw coffins encased in clay or gravel ({{ill|clay coffins|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}}, gravel cists) or buried directly into grave pits. {{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄}} established the connection between waritakegata coffins and burial facilities, and they are considered a defining feature of standardized early kofun burials.Morishta (2005), pp. 55–56. {{ill|Yoshirō Kondō|ja|近藤義郎}} proposed that {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} served as ritual sites for the succession of chieftain spirits, highlighting: # A significant leap in mound shape and scale. # The presence of waritakegata coffins and pit-style stone chambers. # Grave goods, including numerous {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}}, following specific norms.Kondō (1986). {{ill|Kazuo Hirose|ja|広瀬和雄}} emphasized the unique features of early kofun, noting, for instance, the pairing of waritakegata coffins with pit-style stone cists in the {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} and {{ill|Moto Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳}}, compared to {{ill|Akatuka Kofun|ja|赤塚古墳}} in Oita, which used box-shaped stone coffins.Hirose (2003), pp. 155–156. The size of the burial chamber often correlated with the size of the coffin.Ōtsuka and Kobayashi, "Kofun Dictionary," 1982, pp. 367–368. Early examples measured 5–8 meters in length but became shorter over time, with late-period coffins averaging 2–3 meters. By the late Kofun period, boat-shaped coffins and large stone coffins gave way to box-shaped wooden coffins. == Notable Tombs and Burial Mounds == '''Yayoi period''' * {{ill|Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja|市三宅東遺跡}} (Late Yayoi, Shiga Prefecture) * {{ill|Hirabaru Burial Site|ja|平原遺跡}} (Late Yayoi, Fukuoka Prefecture) '''Kofun period''' * {{ill|Eimoridzuka Tumulus 2|ja|衛守塚古墳群}} (Early, Yamagata Prefecture) * {{ill|Tōmimizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳}} (Late Early, Miyagi Prefecture) * {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} (Early, Nara Prefecture) == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Kofun Dictionary|last=Ōtsuka|coauthors=Kobayashi|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|year=1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|title=The Birth of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Kondō|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|year=1986|isbn=4-00-010266-4}} * {{Cite book|title=The State of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Hirose|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=2003|isbn=4-04-703355-3}} == External Links == * Early examples measured 5–8 meters in length but became shorter over time, with late-period coffins averaging 2–3 meters. By the late Kofun period, boat-shaped coffins and large stone coffins gave way to box-shaped wooden coffins. == Notable Tombs and Burial Mounds == '''Yayoi period''' * {{ill|Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja|市三宅東遺跡}} (Late Yayoi, Shiga Prefecture) * {{ill|Hirabaru Burial Site|ja|平原遺跡}} (Late Yayoi, Fukuoka Prefecture) '''Kofun period''' * {{ill|Eimoridzuka Tumulus 2|ja|衛守塚古墳群}} (Early, Yamagata Prefecture) * {{ill|Tōmimizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳}} (Late Early, Miyagi Prefecture) * {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} (Early, Nara Prefecture) == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Kofun Dictionary|last=Ōtsuka|coauthors=Kobayashi|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|year=1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|title=The Birth of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Kondō|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|year=1986|isbn=4-00-010266-4}} * {{Cite book|title=The State of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Hirose|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=2003|isbn=4-04-703355-3}} == External Links == * [[Category:前方後円墳]][[sn:Waritakegata Mokkan]][[Category:前方後円墳]] [[Category:前方後円墳]][[sn:Waritakegata Mokkan]][[Category:前方後円墳]] [[Category:前方後円墳]][[sn:Waritakegata Mokkan]][[Category:前方後円墳]] [[ja:割竹形木棺]]

[[File:Kuroduka kofun museum02s3872.jpg|200px|right|thumb|{{ill|Cinnabar|ja|辰砂}}-coated {{ill|Waritakegata wooden coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan}} replica inside the stone chamber of the {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}}. Visible are the base of the coffin and the {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirror|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}}.]] '''Waritakegata Wooden Coffin''' (割竹形木棺, *Waritakegata Mokukan*) refers to a type of hollowed-out wooden coffin commonly found during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. == Overview == This type of coffin is made by splitting a log lengthwise, hollowing out its interior to create the coffin body and lid, and forming an elongated cylindrical shape. It was often placed inside a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}}.
The waritakegata wooden coffin is believed to have been conceived as a solemn burial vessel for the chieftain class and privileged elites buried in ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}}.
In well-preserved examples, the interior is divided into three sections: the deceased is placed in the central section, with grave goods arranged in the head and foot sections. The coffins were often placed on clay beds, gravel layers, or in chambers equipped with drainage systems, indicating meticulous construction of the burial facilities. Red pigments such as {{ill|Bengala|lt=red ochre|ja|ベンガラ}} (iron(III) oxide, Fe₂O₃) or cinnabar (mercury(II) sulfide, HgS) were sometimes applied to the interior and exterior of the coffin or the walls of the stone chamber. These acts are considered to have had ritual significance.Yoshitaka Hōjō, "Pit-style stone chambers and burial rituals," in {{ill|Makoto Sahara|ja|佐原真}}, 2007, pp. 451–453. == Construction and Materials == The coffins are made by hollowing out giant trees 5 to 8 meters in length and approximately 1 meter in diameter, resulting in the "waritakegata" (split-bamboo) shape. Both ends of the cylindrical coffin were sealed with wooden boards.
In addition to the waritakegata type, there were {{ill|boat-shaped wooden coffins|ja|舟形木棺}}, named for their resemblance to boat prows. These are less commonly found. Most of the source wood for the waritakegata coffins, especially west of the {{ill|Kinki region|ja|近畿地方}}, was evergreen conifers such as {{ill|Sciadopitys verticillata|lt=Kōyamaki|ja|コウヤマキ}}, whereas regions east of {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}} used {{ill|cedar|ja|スギ}}, {{ill|zelkova|ja|ケヤキ}}, and {{ill|hinoki cypress|ja|ヒノキ}}.The {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} contained a coffin made from {{ill|mulberry|ja|クワ}} wood, suggesting the interred may have been connected to sericulture or possibly a female ruler.Examples include cedar from the Ichimiyake Higashi Site (Shiga Prefecture), hinoki from Hitachi Kagami Tumulus (Ibaraki Prefecture), and zelkova from Eimoridzuka Tumulus 2 (Yamagata Prefecture). Few coffins survive intact due to decay, and many structural details remain unknown.The shape of the coffin's bottom is often preserved as an arc-shaped trace on the coffin bed, even when the coffin itself has decayed. == Timeframe and Scale == Waritakegata coffins appeared in the early Kofun period, sometimes even in late {{ill|Yayoi tumulus burials|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs}}. While originally placed inside pit-style stone chambers or stone cists, later periods saw coffins encased in clay or gravel ({{ill|clay coffins|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}}, gravel cists) or buried directly into grave pits. {{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄}} established the connection between waritakegata coffins and burial facilities, and they are considered a defining feature of standardized early kofun burials.Morishta (2005), pp. 55–56. {{ill|Yoshirō Kondō|ja|近藤義郎}} proposed that {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} served as ritual sites for the succession of chieftain spirits, highlighting: # A significant leap in mound shape and scale. # The presence of waritakegata coffins and pit-style stone chambers. # Grave goods, including numerous {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}}, following specific norms.Kondō (1986). {{ill|Kazuo Hirose|ja|広瀬和雄}} emphasized the unique features of early kofun, noting, for instance, the pairing of waritakegata coffins with pit-style stone cists in the {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} and {{ill|Moto Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳}}, compared to {{ill|Akatuka Kofun|ja|赤塚古墳}} in Oita, which used box-shaped stone coffins.Hirose (2003), pp. 155–156. The size of the burial chamber often correlated with the size of the coffin.Ōtsuka and Kobayashi, "Kofun Dictionary," 1982, pp. 367–368. Early examples measured 5–8 meters in length but became shorter over time, with late-period coffins averaging 2–3 meters. By the late Kofun period, boat-shaped coffins and large stone coffins gave way to box-shaped wooden coffins. == Notable Tombs and Burial Mounds == '''Yayoi period''' * {{ill|Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja|市三宅東遺跡}} (Late Yayoi, Shiga Prefecture) * {{ill|Hirabaru Burial Site|ja|平原遺跡}} (Late Yayoi, Fukuoka Prefecture) '''Kofun period''' * {{ill|Eimoridzuka Tumulus 2|ja|衛守塚古墳群}} (Early, Yamagata Prefecture) * {{ill|Tōmimizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳}} (Late Early, Miyagi Prefecture) * {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} (Early, Nara Prefecture) == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Kofun Dictionary|last=Ōtsuka|coauthors=Kobayashi|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|year=1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|title=The Birth of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Kondō|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|year=1986|isbn=4-00-010266-4}} * {{Cite book|title=The State of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Hirose|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=2003|isbn=4-04-703355-3}} == External Links == * [[File:Kuroduka kofun museum02s3872.jpg|200px|right|thumb|{{ill|Cinnabar|ja|辰砂}}-coated {{ill|Waritakegata wooden coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan}} replica inside the stone chamber of the {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}}. Visible are the base of the coffin and the {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirror|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}}.]] '''Waritakegata Wooden Coffin''' (割竹形木棺, *Waritakegata Mokukan*) refers to a type of hollowed-out wooden coffin commonly found during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. == Overview == This type of coffin is made by splitting a log lengthwise, hollowing out its interior to create the coffin body and lid, and forming an elongated cylindrical shape. It was often placed inside a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}}.
The waritakegata wooden coffin is believed to have been conceived as a solemn burial vessel for the chieftain class and privileged elites buried in ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}}.
In well-preserved examples, the interior is divided into three sections: the deceased is placed in the central section, with grave goods arranged in the head and foot sections. The coffins were often placed on clay beds, gravel layers, or in chambers equipped with drainage systems, indicating meticulous construction of the burial facilities. Red pigments such as {{ill|Bengala|lt=red ochre|ja|ベンガラ}} (iron(III) oxide, Fe₂O₃) or cinnabar (mercury(II) sulfide, HgS) were sometimes applied to the interior and exterior of the coffin or the walls of the stone chamber. These acts are considered to have had ritual significance.Yoshitaka Hōjō, "Pit-style stone chambers and burial rituals," in {{ill|Makoto Sahara|ja|佐原真}}, 2007, pp. 451–453. == Construction and Materials == The coffins are made by hollowing out giant trees 5 to 8 meters in length and approximately 1 meter in diameter, resulting in the "waritakegata" (split-bamboo) shape. Both ends of the cylindrical coffin were sealed with wooden boards.
In addition to the waritakegata type, there were {{ill|boat-shaped wooden coffins|ja|舟形木棺}}, named for their resemblance to boat prows. These are less commonly found. Most of the source wood for the waritakegata coffins, especially west of the {{ill|Kinki region|ja|近畿地方}}, was evergreen conifers such as {{ill|Sciadopitys verticillata|lt=Kōyamaki|ja|コウヤマキ}}, whereas regions east of {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}} used {{ill|cedar|ja|スギ}}, {{ill|zelkova|ja|ケヤキ}}, and {{ill|hinoki cypress|ja|ヒノキ}}.The {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} contained a coffin made from {{ill|mulberry|ja|クワ}} wood, suggesting the interred may have been connected to sericulture or possibly a female ruler.Examples include cedar from the Ichimiyake Higashi Site (Shiga Prefecture), hinoki from Hitachi Kagami Tumulus (Ibaraki Prefecture), and zelkova from Eimoridzuka Tumulus 2 (Yamagata Prefecture). Few coffins survive intact due to decay, and many structural details remain unknown.The shape of the coffin's bottom is often preserved as an arc-shaped trace on the coffin bed, even when the coffin itself has decayed. == Timeframe and Scale == Waritakegata coffins appeared in the early Kofun period, sometimes even in late {{ill|Yayoi tumulus burials|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs}}. While originally placed inside pit-style stone chambers or stone cists, later periods saw coffins encased in clay or gravel ({{ill|clay coffins|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}}, gravel cists) or buried directly into grave pits. {{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄}} established the connection between waritakegata coffins and burial facilities, and they are considered a defining feature of standardized early kofun burials.Morishta (2005), pp. 55–56. {{ill|Yoshirō Kondō|ja|近藤義郎}} proposed that {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} served as ritual sites for the succession of chieftain spirits, highlighting: # A significant leap in mound shape and scale. # The presence of waritakegata coffins and pit-style stone chambers. # Grave goods, including numerous {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}}, following specific norms.Kondō (1986). {{ill|Kazuo Hirose|ja|広瀬和雄}} emphasized the unique features of early kofun, noting, for instance, the pairing of waritakegata coffins with pit-style stone cists in the {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} and {{ill|Moto Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳}}, compared to {{ill|Akatuka Kofun|ja|赤塚古墳}} in Oita, which used box-shaped stone coffins.Hirose (2003), pp. 155–156. The size of the burial chamber often correlated with the size of the coffin.Ōtsuka and Kobayashi, "Kofun Dictionary," 1982, pp. 367–368. Early examples measured 5–8 meters in length but became shorter over time, with late-period coffins averaging 2–3 meters. By the late Kofun period, boat-shaped coffins and large stone coffins gave way to box-shaped wooden coffins. == Notable Tombs and Burial Mounds == '''Yayoi period''' * {{ill|Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja|市三宅東遺跡}} (Late Yayoi, Shiga Prefecture) * {{ill|Hirabaru Burial Site|ja|平原遺跡}} (Late Yayoi, Fukuoka Prefecture) '''Kofun period''' * {{ill|Eimoridzuka Tumulus 2|ja|衛守塚古墳群}} (Early, Yamagata Prefecture) * {{ill|Tōmimizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳}} (Late Early, Miyagi Prefecture) * {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} (Early, Nara Prefecture) == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Kofun Dictionary|last=Ōtsuka|coauthors=Kobayashi|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|year=1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|title=The Birth of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Kondō|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|year=1986|isbn=4-00-010266-4}} * {{Cite book|title=The State of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Hirose|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=2003|isbn=4-04-703355-3}} == External Links == * [[ja:割竹形木棺]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Waritakegata Mokkan]][[Category:qq]]

[[File:Kuroduka kofun museum02s3872.jpg|200px|right|thumb|{{ill|Cinnabar|ja|辰砂}}-coated {{ill|Waritakegata wooden coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan}} replica inside the stone chamber of the {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}}. Visible are the base of the coffin and the {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirror|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}}.]] '''Waritakegata Wooden Coffin''' (割竹形木棺, *Waritakegata Mokukan*) refers to a type of hollowed-out wooden coffin commonly found during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. == Overview == This type of coffin is made by splitting a log lengthwise, hollowing out its interior to create the coffin body and lid, and forming an elongated cylindrical shape. It was often placed inside a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}}.
The waritakegata wooden coffin is believed to have been conceived as a solemn burial vessel for the chieftain class and privileged elites buried in ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}}.
In well-preserved examples, the interior is divided into three sections: the deceased is placed in the central section, with grave goods arranged in the head and foot sections. The coffins were often placed on clay beds, gravel layers, or in chambers equipped with drainage systems, indicating meticulous construction of the burial facilities. Red pigments such as {{ill|Bengala|lt=red ochre|ja|ベンガラ}} (iron(III) oxide, Fe₂O₃) or cinnabar (mercury(II) sulfide, HgS) were sometimes applied to the interior and exterior of the coffin or the walls of the stone chamber. These acts are considered to have had ritual significance.Yoshitaka Hōjō, "Pit-style stone chambers and burial rituals," in {{ill|Makoto Sahara|ja|佐原真}}, 2007, pp. 451–453. == Construction and Materials == The coffins are made by hollowing out giant trees 5 to 8 meters in length and approximately 1 meter in diameter, resulting in the "waritakegata" (split-bamboo) shape. Both ends of the cylindrical coffin were sealed with wooden boards.
In addition to the waritakegata type, there were {{ill|boat-shaped wooden coffins|ja|舟形木棺}}, named for their resemblance to boat prows. These are less commonly found. Most of the source wood for the waritakegata coffins, especially west of the {{ill|Kinki region|ja|近畿地方}}, was evergreen conifers such as {{ill|Sciadopitys verticillata|lt=Kōyamaki|ja|コウヤマキ}}, whereas regions east of {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}} used {{ill|cedar|ja|スギ}}, {{ill|zelkova|ja|ケヤキ}}, and {{ill|hinoki cypress|ja|ヒノキ}}.The {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} contained a coffin made from {{ill|mulberry|ja|クワ}} wood, suggesting the interred may have been connected to sericulture or possibly a female ruler.Examples include cedar from the Ichimiyake Higashi Site (Shiga Prefecture), hinoki from Hitachi Kagami Tumulus (Ibaraki Prefecture), and zelkova from Eimoridzuka Tumulus 2 (Yamagata Prefecture). Few coffins survive intact due to decay, and many structural details remain unknown.The shape of the coffin's bottom is often preserved as an arc-shaped trace on the coffin bed, even when the coffin itself has decayed. == Timeframe and Scale == Waritakegata coffins appeared in the early Kofun period, sometimes even in late {{ill|Yayoi tumulus burials|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs}}. While originally placed inside pit-style stone chambers or stone cists, later periods saw coffins encased in clay or gravel ({{ill|clay coffins|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}}, gravel cists) or buried directly into grave pits. {{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄}} established the connection between waritakegata coffins and burial facilities, and they are considered a defining feature of standardized early kofun burials.Morishta (2005), pp. 55–56. {{ill|Yoshirō Kondō|ja|近藤義郎}} proposed that {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} served as ritual sites for the succession of chieftain spirits, highlighting: # A significant leap in mound shape and scale. # The presence of waritakegata coffins and pit-style stone chambers. # Grave goods, including numerous {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}}, following specific norms.Kondō (1986). {{ill|Kazuo Hirose|ja|広瀬和雄}} emphasized the unique features of early kofun, noting, for instance, the pairing of waritakegata coffins with pit-style stone cists in the {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} and {{ill|Moto Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳}}, compared to {{ill|Akatuka Kofun|ja|赤塚古墳}} in Oita, which used box-shaped stone coffins.Hirose (2003), pp. 155–156. The size of the burial chamber often correlated with the size of the coffin.Ōtsuka and Kobayashi, "Kofun Dictionary," 1982, pp. 367–368. Early examples measured 5–8 meters in length but became shorter over time, with late-period coffins averaging 2–3 meters. By the late Kofun period, boat-shaped coffins and large stone coffins gave way to box-shaped wooden coffins. == Notable Tombs and Burial Mounds == '''Yayoi period''' * {{ill|Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja|市三宅東遺跡}} (Late Yayoi, Shiga Prefecture) * {{ill|Hirabaru Burial Site|ja|平原遺跡}} (Late Yayoi, Fukuoka Prefecture) '''Kofun period''' * {{ill|Eimoridzuka Tumulus 2|ja|衛守塚古墳群}} (Early, Yamagata Prefecture) * {{ill|Tōmimizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳}} (Late Early, Miyagi Prefecture) * {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} (Early, Nara Prefecture) == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Kofun Dictionary|last=Ōtsuka|coauthors=Kobayashi|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|year=1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|title=The Birth of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Kondō|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|year=1986|isbn=4-00-010266-4}} * {{Cite book|title=The State of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Hirose|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=2003|isbn=4-04-703355-3}} == External Links == * Most of the source wood for the waritakegata coffins, especially west of the {{ill|Kinki region|ja|近畿地方}}, was evergreen conifers such as {{ill|Sciadopitys verticillata|lt=Kōyamaki|ja|コウヤマキ}}, whereas regions east of {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}} used {{ill|cedar|ja|スギ}}, {{ill|zelkova|ja|ケヤキ}}, and {{ill|hinoki cypress|ja|ヒノキ}}.The {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} contained a coffin made from {{ill|mulberry|ja|クワ}} wood, suggesting the interred may have been connected to sericulture or possibly a female ruler.Examples include cedar from the Ichimiyake Higashi Site (Shiga Prefecture), hinoki from Hitachi Kagami Tumulus (Ibaraki Prefecture), and zelkova from Eimoridzuka Tumulus 2 (Yamagata Prefecture). Few coffins survive intact due to decay, and many structural details remain unknown.The shape of the coffin's bottom is often preserved as an arc-shaped trace on the coffin bed, even when the coffin itself has decayed. == Timeframe and Scale == Waritakegata coffins appeared in the early Kofun period, sometimes even in late {{ill|Yayoi tumulus burials|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs}}. While originally placed inside pit-style stone chambers or stone cists, later periods saw coffins encased in clay or gravel ({{ill|clay coffins|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}}, gravel cists) or buried directly into grave pits. {{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄}} established the connection between waritakegata coffins and burial facilities, and they are considered a defining feature of standardized early kofun burials.Morishta (2005), pp. 55–56. {{ill|Yoshirō Kondō|ja|近藤義郎}} proposed that {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} served as ritual sites for the succession of chieftain spirits, highlighting: # A significant leap in mound shape and scale. # The presence of waritakegata coffins and pit-style stone chambers. # Grave goods, including numerous {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}}, following specific norms.Kondō (1986). {{ill|Kazuo Hirose|ja|広瀬和雄}} emphasized the unique features of early kofun, noting, for instance, the pairing of waritakegata coffins with pit-style stone cists in the {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} and {{ill|Moto Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳}}, compared to {{ill|Akatuka Kofun|ja|赤塚古墳}} in Oita, which used box-shaped stone coffins.Hirose (2003), pp. 155–156. The size of the burial chamber often correlated with the size of the coffin.Ōtsuka and Kobayashi, "Kofun Dictionary," 1982, pp. 367–368. Early examples measured 5–8 meters in length but became shorter over time, with late-period coffins averaging 2–3 meters. By the late Kofun period, boat-shaped coffins and large stone coffins gave way to box-shaped wooden coffins. == Notable Tombs and Burial Mounds == '''Yayoi period''' * {{ill|Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja|市三宅東遺跡}} (Late Yayoi, Shiga Prefecture) * {{ill|Hirabaru Burial Site|ja|平原遺跡}} (Late Yayoi, Fukuoka Prefecture) '''Kofun period''' * {{ill|Eimoridzuka Tumulus 2|ja|衛守塚古墳群}} (Early, Yamagata Prefecture) * {{ill|Tōmimizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳}} (Late Early, Miyagi Prefecture) * {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} (Early, Nara Prefecture) == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Kofun Dictionary|last=Ōtsuka|coauthors=Kobayashi|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|year=1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|title=The Birth of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Kondō|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|year=1986|isbn=4-00-010266-4}} * {{Cite book|title=The State of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Hirose|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=2003|isbn=4-04-703355-3}} == External Links == * [[File:Kuroduka kofun museum02s3872.jpg|200px|right|thumb|{{ill|Cinnabar|ja|辰砂}}-coated {{ill|Waritakegata wooden coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan}} replica inside the stone chamber of the {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}}. Visible are the base of the coffin and the {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirror|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}}.]] '''Waritakegata Wooden Coffin''' (割竹形木棺, *Waritakegata Mokukan*) refers to a type of hollowed-out wooden coffin commonly found during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. == Overview == This type of coffin is made by splitting a log lengthwise, hollowing out its interior to create the coffin body and lid, and forming an elongated cylindrical shape. It was often placed inside a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}}.
The waritakegata wooden coffin is believed to have been conceived as a solemn burial vessel for the chieftain class and privileged elites buried in ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}}.
In well-preserved examples, the interior is divided into three sections: the deceased is placed in the central section, with grave goods arranged in the head and foot sections. The coffins were often placed on clay beds, gravel layers, or in chambers equipped with drainage systems, indicating meticulous construction of the burial facilities. Red pigments such as {{ill|Bengala|lt=red ochre|ja|ベンガラ}} (iron(III) oxide, Fe₂O₃) or cinnabar (mercury(II) sulfide, HgS) were sometimes applied to the interior and exterior of the coffin or the walls of the stone chamber. These acts are considered to have had ritual significance.Yoshitaka Hōjō, "Pit-style stone chambers and burial rituals," in {{ill|Makoto Sahara|ja|佐原真}}, 2007, pp. 451–453. == Construction and Materials == The coffins are made by hollowing out giant trees 5 to 8 meters in length and approximately 1 meter in diameter, resulting in the "waritakegata" (split-bamboo) shape. Both ends of the cylindrical coffin were sealed with wooden boards.
In addition to the waritakegata type, there were {{ill|boat-shaped wooden coffins|ja|舟形木棺}}, named for their resemblance to boat prows. These are less commonly found. Most of the source wood for the waritakegata coffins, especially west of the {{ill|Kinki region|ja|近畿地方}}, was evergreen conifers such as {{ill|Sciadopitys verticillata|lt=Kōyamaki|ja|コウヤマキ}}, whereas regions east of {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}} used {{ill|cedar|ja|スギ}}, {{ill|zelkova|ja|ケヤキ}}, and {{ill|hinoki cypress|ja|ヒノキ}}.The {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} contained a coffin made from {{ill|mulberry|ja|クワ}} wood, suggesting the interred may have been connected to sericulture or possibly a female ruler.Examples include cedar from the Ichimiyake Higashi Site (Shiga Prefecture), hinoki from Hitachi Kagami Tumulus (Ibaraki Prefecture), and zelkova from Eimoridzuka Tumulus 2 (Yamagata Prefecture). Few coffins survive intact due to decay, and many structural details remain unknown.The shape of the coffin's bottom is often preserved as an arc-shaped trace on the coffin bed, even when the coffin itself has decayed. == Timeframe and Scale == Waritakegata coffins appeared in the early Kofun period, sometimes even in late {{ill|Yayoi tumulus burials|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs}}. While originally placed inside pit-style stone chambers or stone cists, later periods saw coffins encased in clay or gravel ({{ill|clay coffins|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}}, gravel cists) or buried directly into grave pits. {{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄}} established the connection between waritakegata coffins and burial facilities, and they are considered a defining feature of standardized early kofun burials.Morishta (2005), pp. 55–56. {{ill|Yoshirō Kondō|ja|近藤義郎}} proposed that {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} served as ritual sites for the succession of chieftain spirits, highlighting: # A significant leap in mound shape and scale. # The presence of waritakegata coffins and pit-style stone chambers. # Grave goods, including numerous {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}}, following specific norms.Kondō (1986). {{ill|Kazuo Hirose|ja|広瀬和雄}} emphasized the unique features of early kofun, noting, for instance, the pairing of waritakegata coffins with pit-style stone cists in the {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} and {{ill|Moto Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳}}, compared to {{ill|Akatuka Kofun|ja|赤塚古墳}} in Oita, which used box-shaped stone coffins.Hirose (2003), pp. 155–156. The size of the burial chamber often correlated with the size of the coffin.Ōtsuka and Kobayashi, "Kofun Dictionary," 1982, pp. 367–368. Early examples measured 5–8 meters in length but became shorter over time, with late-period coffins averaging 2–3 meters. By the late Kofun period, boat-shaped coffins and large stone coffins gave way to box-shaped wooden coffins. == Notable Tombs and Burial Mounds == '''Yayoi period''' * {{ill|Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja|市三宅東遺跡}} (Late Yayoi, Shiga Prefecture) * {{ill|Hirabaru Burial Site|ja|平原遺跡}} (Late Yayoi, Fukuoka Prefecture) '''Kofun period''' * {{ill|Eimoridzuka Tumulus 2|ja|衛守塚古墳群}} (Early, Yamagata Prefecture) * {{ill|Tōmimizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳}} (Late Early, Miyagi Prefecture) * {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} (Early, Nara Prefecture) == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Kofun Dictionary|last=Ōtsuka|coauthors=Kobayashi|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|year=1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|title=The Birth of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Kondō|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|year=1986|isbn=4-00-010266-4}} * {{Cite book|title=The State of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Hirose|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=2003|isbn=4-04-703355-3}} == External Links == * [[File:Kuroduka kofun museum02s3872.jpg|200px|right|thumb|{{ill|Cinnabar|ja|辰砂}}-coated {{ill|Waritakegata wooden coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan}} replica inside the stone chamber of the {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}}. Visible are the base of the coffin and the {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirror|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}}.]] '''Waritakegata Wooden Coffin''' (割竹形木棺, *Waritakegata Mokukan*) refers to a type of hollowed-out wooden coffin commonly found during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. == Overview == This type of coffin is made by splitting a log lengthwise, hollowing out its interior to create the coffin body and lid, and forming an elongated cylindrical shape. It was often placed inside a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}}.
The waritakegata wooden coffin is believed to have been conceived as a solemn burial vessel for the chieftain class and privileged elites buried in ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}}.
In well-preserved examples, the interior is divided into three sections: the deceased is placed in the central section, with grave goods arranged in the head and foot sections. The coffins were often placed on clay beds, gravel layers, or in chambers equipped with drainage systems, indicating meticulous construction of the burial facilities. Red pigments such as {{ill|Bengala|lt=red ochre|ja|ベンガラ}} (iron(III) oxide, Fe₂O₃) or cinnabar (mercury(II) sulfide, HgS) were sometimes applied to the interior and exterior of the coffin or the walls of the stone chamber. These acts are considered to have had ritual significance.Yoshitaka Hōjō, "Pit-style stone chambers and burial rituals," in {{ill|Makoto Sahara|ja|佐原真}}, 2007, pp. 451–453. == Construction and Materials == The coffins are made by hollowing out giant trees 5 to 8 meters in length and approximately 1 meter in diameter, resulting in the "waritakegata" (split-bamboo) shape. Both ends of the cylindrical coffin were sealed with wooden boards.
In addition to the waritakegata type, there were {{ill|boat-shaped wooden coffins|ja|舟形木棺}}, named for their resemblance to boat prows. These are less commonly found. Most of the source wood for the waritakegata coffins, especially west of the {{ill|Kinki region|ja|近畿地方}}, was evergreen conifers such as {{ill|Sciadopitys verticillata|lt=Kōyamaki|ja|コウヤマキ}}, whereas regions east of {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}} used {{ill|cedar|ja|スギ}}, {{ill|zelkova|ja|ケヤキ}}, and {{ill|hinoki cypress|ja|ヒノキ}}.The {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} contained a coffin made from {{ill|mulberry|ja|クワ}} wood, suggesting the interred may have been connected to sericulture or possibly a female ruler.Examples include cedar from the Ichimiyake Higashi Site (Shiga Prefecture), hinoki from Hitachi Kagami Tumulus (Ibaraki Prefecture), and zelkova from Eimoridzuka Tumulus 2 (Yamagata Prefecture). Few coffins survive intact due to decay, and many structural details remain unknown.The shape of the coffin's bottom is often preserved as an arc-shaped trace on the coffin bed, even when the coffin itself has decayed. == Timeframe and Scale == Waritakegata coffins appeared in the early Kofun period, sometimes even in late {{ill|Yayoi tumulus burials|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs}}. While originally placed inside pit-style stone chambers or stone cists, later periods saw coffins encased in clay or gravel ({{ill|clay coffins|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}}, gravel cists) or buried directly into grave pits. {{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄}} established the connection between waritakegata coffins and burial facilities, and they are considered a defining feature of standardized early kofun burials.Morishta (2005), pp. 55–56. {{ill|Yoshirō Kondō|ja|近藤義郎}} proposed that {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} served as ritual sites for the succession of chieftain spirits, highlighting: # A significant leap in mound shape and scale. # The presence of waritakegata coffins and pit-style stone chambers. # Grave goods, including numerous {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}}, following specific norms.Kondō (1986). {{ill|Kazuo Hirose|ja|広瀬和雄}} emphasized the unique features of early kofun, noting, for instance, the pairing of waritakegata coffins with pit-style stone cists in the {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} and {{ill|Moto Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳}}, compared to {{ill|Akatuka Kofun|ja|赤塚古墳}} in Oita, which used box-shaped stone coffins.Hirose (2003), pp. 155–156. The size of the burial chamber often correlated with the size of the coffin.Ōtsuka and Kobayashi, "Kofun Dictionary," 1982, pp. 367–368. Early examples measured 5–8 meters in length but became shorter over time, with late-period coffins averaging 2–3 meters. By the late Kofun period, boat-shaped coffins and large stone coffins gave way to box-shaped wooden coffins. == Notable Tombs and Burial Mounds == '''Yayoi period''' * {{ill|Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja|市三宅東遺跡}} (Late Yayoi, Shiga Prefecture) * {{ill|Hirabaru Burial Site|ja|平原遺跡}} (Late Yayoi, Fukuoka Prefecture) '''Kofun period''' * {{ill|Eimoridzuka Tumulus 2|ja|衛守塚古墳群}} (Early, Yamagata Prefecture) * {{ill|Tōmimizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳}} (Late Early, Miyagi Prefecture) * {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} (Early, Nara Prefecture) == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Kofun Dictionary|last=Ōtsuka|coauthors=Kobayashi|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|year=1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|title=The Birth of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Kondō|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|year=1986|isbn=4-00-010266-4}} * {{Cite book|title=The State of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Hirose|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=2003|isbn=4-04-703355-3}} == External Links == * [[File:Kuroduka kofun museum02s3872.jpg|200px|right|thumb|{{ill|Cinnabar|ja|辰砂}}-coated {{ill|Waritakegata wooden coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan}} replica inside the stone chamber of the {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}}. Visible are the base of the coffin and the {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirror|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}}.]] '''Waritakegata Wooden Coffin''' (割竹形木棺, *Waritakegata Mokukan*) refers to a type of hollowed-out wooden coffin commonly found during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. == Overview == This type of coffin is made by splitting a log lengthwise, hollowing out its interior to create the coffin body and lid, and forming an elongated cylindrical shape. It was often placed inside a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}}.
The waritakegata wooden coffin is believed to have been conceived as a solemn burial vessel for the chieftain class and privileged elites buried in ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}}.
In well-preserved examples, the interior is divided into three sections: the deceased is placed in the central section, with grave goods arranged in the head and foot sections. The coffins were often placed on clay beds, gravel layers, or in chambers equipped with drainage systems, indicating meticulous construction of the burial facilities. Red pigments such as {{ill|Bengala|lt=red ochre|ja|ベンガラ}} (iron(III) oxide, Fe₂O₃) or cinnabar (mercury(II) sulfide, HgS) were sometimes applied to the interior and exterior of the coffin or the walls of the stone chamber. These acts are considered to have had ritual significance.Yoshitaka Hōjō, "Pit-style stone chambers and burial rituals," in {{ill|Makoto Sahara|ja|佐原真}}, 2007, pp. 451–453. == Construction and Materials == The coffins are made by hollowing out giant trees 5 to 8 meters in length and approximately 1 meter in diameter, resulting in the "waritakegata" (split-bamboo) shape. Both ends of the cylindrical coffin were sealed with wooden boards.
In addition to the waritakegata type, there were {{ill|boat-shaped wooden coffins|ja|舟形木棺}}, named for their resemblance to boat prows. These are less commonly found. Most of the source wood for the waritakegata coffins, especially west of the {{ill|Kinki region|ja|近畿地方}}, was evergreen conifers such as {{ill|Sciadopitys verticillata|lt=Kōyamaki|ja|コウヤマキ}}, whereas regions east of {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}} used {{ill|cedar|ja|スギ}}, {{ill|zelkova|ja|ケヤキ}}, and {{ill|hinoki cypress|ja|ヒノキ}}.The {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} contained a coffin made from {{ill|mulberry|ja|クワ}} wood, suggesting the interred may have been connected to sericulture or possibly a female ruler.Examples include cedar from the Ichimiyake Higashi Site (Shiga Prefecture), hinoki from Hitachi Kagami Tumulus (Ibaraki Prefecture), and zelkova from Eimoridzuka Tumulus 2 (Yamagata Prefecture). Few coffins survive intact due to decay, and many structural details remain unknown.The shape of the coffin's bottom is often preserved as an arc-shaped trace on the coffin bed, even when the coffin itself has decayed. == Timeframe and Scale == Waritakegata coffins appeared in the early Kofun period, sometimes even in late {{ill|Yayoi tumulus burials|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs}}. While originally placed inside pit-style stone chambers or stone cists, later periods saw coffins encased in clay or gravel ({{ill|clay coffins|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}}, gravel cists) or buried directly into grave pits. {{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄}} established the connection between waritakegata coffins and burial facilities, and they are considered a defining feature of standardized early kofun burials.Morishta (2005), pp. 55–56. {{ill|Yoshirō Kondō|ja|近藤義郎}} proposed that {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} served as ritual sites for the succession of chieftain spirits, highlighting: # A significant leap in mound shape and scale. # The presence of waritakegata coffins and pit-style stone chambers. # Grave goods, including numerous {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}}, following specific norms.Kondō (1986). {{ill|Kazuo Hirose|ja|広瀬和雄}} emphasized the unique features of early kofun, noting, for instance, the pairing of waritakegata coffins with pit-style stone cists in the {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} and {{ill|Moto Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳}}, compared to {{ill|Akatuka Kofun|ja|赤塚古墳}} in Oita, which used box-shaped stone coffins.Hirose (2003), pp. 155–156. The size of the burial chamber often correlated with the size of the coffin.Ōtsuka and Kobayashi, "Kofun Dictionary," 1982, pp. 367–368. Early examples measured 5–8 meters in length but became shorter over time, with late-period coffins averaging 2–3 meters. By the late Kofun period, boat-shaped coffins and large stone coffins gave way to box-shaped wooden coffins. == Notable Tombs and Burial Mounds == '''Yayoi period''' * {{ill|Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja|市三宅東遺跡}} (Late Yayoi, Shiga Prefecture) * {{ill|Hirabaru Burial Site|ja|平原遺跡}} (Late Yayoi, Fukuoka Prefecture) '''Kofun period''' * {{ill|Eimoridzuka Tumulus 2|ja|衛守塚古墳群}} (Early, Yamagata Prefecture) * {{ill|Tōmimizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳}} (Late Early, Miyagi Prefecture) * {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} (Early, Nara Prefecture) == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Kofun Dictionary|last=Ōtsuka|coauthors=Kobayashi|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|year=1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|title=The Birth of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Kondō|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|year=1986|isbn=4-00-010266-4}} * {{Cite book|title=The State of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Hirose|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=2003|isbn=4-04-703355-3}} == External Links == * Prefecture) * {{ill|Tōmimizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳}} (Late Early, Miyagi Prefecture) * {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} (Early, Nara Prefecture) == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Kofun Dictionary|last=Ōtsuka|coauthors=Kobayashi|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|year=1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|title=The Birth of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Kondō|publisher=Iwanami [[File:Kuroduka kofun museum02s3872.jpg|200px|right|thumb|{{ill|Cinnabar|ja|辰砂}}-coated {{ill|Waritakegata wooden coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan}} replica inside the stone chamber of the {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}}. Visible are the base of the coffin and the {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirror|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}}.]] '''Waritakegata Wooden Coffin''' (割竹形木棺, *Waritakegata Mokukan*) refers to a type of hollowed-out wooden coffin commonly found during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. == Overview == This type of coffin is made by splitting a log lengthwise, hollowing out its interior to create the coffin body and lid, and forming an elongated cylindrical shape. It was often placed inside a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}}.
The waritakegata wooden coffin is believed to have been conceived as a solemn burial vessel for the chieftain class and privileged elites buried in ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}}.
In well-preserved examples, the interior is divided into three sections: the deceased is placed in the central section, with grave goods arranged in the head and foot sections. The coffins were often placed on clay beds, gravel layers, or in chambers equipped with drainage systems, indicating meticulous construction of the burial facilities. Red pigments such as {{ill|Bengala|lt=red ochre|ja|ベンガラ}} (iron(III) oxide, Fe₂O₃) or cinnabar (mercury(II) sulfide, HgS) were sometimes applied to the interior and exterior of the coffin or the walls of the stone chamber. These acts are considered to have had ritual significance.Yoshitaka Hōjō, "Pit-style stone chambers and burial rituals," in {{ill|Makoto Sahara|ja|佐原真}}, 2007, pp. 451–453. == Construction and Materials == The coffins are made by hollowing out giant trees 5 to 8 meters in length and approximately 1 meter in diameter, resulting in the "waritakegata" (split-bamboo) shape. Both ends of the cylindrical coffin were sealed with wooden boards.
In addition to the waritakegata type, there were {{ill|boat-shaped wooden coffins|ja|舟形木棺}}, named for their resemblance to boat prows. These are less commonly found. Most of the source wood for the waritakegata coffins, especially west of the {{ill|Kinki region|ja|近畿地方}}, was evergreen conifers such as {{ill|Sciadopitys verticillata|lt=Kōyamaki|ja|コウヤマキ}}, whereas regions east of {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}} used {{ill|cedar|ja|スギ}}, {{ill|zelkova|ja|ケヤキ}}, and {{ill|hinoki cypress|ja|ヒノキ}}.The {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} contained a coffin made from {{ill|mulberry|ja|クワ}} wood, suggesting the interred may have been connected to sericulture or possibly a female ruler.Examples include cedar from the Ichimiyake Higashi Site (Shiga Prefecture), hinoki from Hitachi Kagami Kofun (Ibaraki Prefecture), and zelkova from Eimoridzuka Kofun 2 (Yamagata Prefecture). Few coffins survive intact due to decay, and many structural details remain unknown.The shape of the coffin's bottom is often preserved as an arc-shaped trace on the coffin bed, even when the coffin itself has decayed. == Timeframe and Scale == Waritakegata coffins appeared in the early Kofun period, sometimes even in late {{ill|Yayoi tumulus burials|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs}}. While originally placed inside pit-style stone chambers or stone cists, later periods saw coffins encased in clay or gravel ({{ill|clay coffins|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}}, gravel cists) or buried directly into grave pits. {{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄}} established the connection between waritakegata coffins and burial facilities, and they are considered a defining feature of standardized early kofun burials.Morishta (2005), pp. 55–56. {{ill|Yoshirō Kondō|ja|近藤義郎}} proposed that {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} served as ritual sites for the succession of chieftain spirits, highlighting: # A significant leap in mound shape and scale. # The presence of waritakegata coffins and pit-style stone chambers. # Grave goods, including numerous {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}}, following specific norms.Kondō (1986). {{ill|Kazuo Hirose|ja|広瀬和雄}} emphasized the unique features of early kofun, noting, for instance, the pairing of waritakegata coffins with pit-style stone cists in the {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} and {{ill|Moto Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳}}, compared to {{ill|Akatuka Kofun|ja|赤塚古墳}} in Oita, which used box-shaped stone coffins.Hirose (2003), pp. 155–156. The size of the burial chamber often correlated with the size of the coffin.Ōtsuka and Kobayashi, "Kofun Dictionary," 1982, pp. 367–368. Early examples measured 5–8 meters in length but became shorter over time, with late-period coffins averaging 2–3 meters. By the late Kofun period, boat-shaped coffins and large stone coffins gave way to box-shaped wooden coffins. == Notable Tombs and Kofuns == '''Yayoi period''' * {{ill|Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja|市三宅東遺跡}} (Late Yayoi, Shiga Prefecture) * {{ill|Hirabaru Burial Site|ja|平原遺跡}} (Late Yayoi, Fukuoka Prefecture) '''Kofun period''' * {{ill|Eimoridzuka Kofun 2|ja|衛守塚古墳群}} (Early, Yamagata Prefecture) * {{ill|Tōmimizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳}} (Late Early, Miyagi Prefecture) * {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} (Early, Nara Prefecture) == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Kofun Dictionary|last=Ōtsuka|coauthors=Kobayashi|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|year=1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|title=The Birth of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Kondō|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|year=1986|isbn=4-00-010266-4}} * {{Cite book|title=The State of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Hirose|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=2003|isbn=4-04-703355-3}} == External Links == * Most of the source wood for the waritakegata coffins, especially west of the {{ill|Kinki region|ja|近畿地方}}, was evergreen conifers such as {{ill|Sciadopitys verticillata|lt=Kōyamaki|ja|コウヤマキ}}, whereas regions east of {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}} used {{ill|cedar|ja|スギ}}, {{ill|zelkova|ja|ケヤキ}}, and {{ill|hinoki cypress|ja|ヒノキ}}.The {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} contained a coffin made from {{ill|mulberry|ja|クワ}} wood, suggesting the interred may have been connected to sericulture or possibly a female ruler.Examples include cedar from the Ichimiyake Higashi Site (Shiga Prefecture), hinoki from Hitachi Kagami Kofun (Ibaraki Prefecture), and zelkova from Eimoridzuka Kofun 2 (Yamagata Prefecture). While originally placed inside pit-style stone chambers or stone cists, later periods saw coffins encased in clay or gravel ({{ill|clay coffins|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}}, gravel cists) or buried directly into grave pits. {{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄}} established the connection between waritakegata coffins and burial facilities, and they are considered a defining feature of standardized early kofun burials.Morishta (2005), pp. 55–56. {{ill|Yoshirō Kondō|ja|近藤義郎}} proposed that {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} served as ritual sites for the succession of chieftain spirits, highlighting: # A significant leap in mound shape and scale. # The presence of waritakegata coffins and pit-style stone chambers. # Grave goods, including numerous {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}}, following specific norms.Kondō (1986). {{ill|Kazuo Hirose|ja|広瀬和雄}} emphasized the unique features of early kofun, noting, for instance, the pairing of waritakegata coffins with pit-style stone cists in the {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} and {{ill|Moto Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳}}, compared to {{ill|Akatuka Kofun|ja|赤塚古墳}} in Oita, which used box-shaped stone coffins.Hirose (2003), pp. 155–156. The size of the burial chamber often correlated with the size of the coffin.Ōtsuka and Kobayashi, "Kofun Dictionary," 1982, pp. 367–368. Early examples measured 5–8 meters in length but became shorter over time, with late-period coffins averaging 2–3 meters. By the late Kofun period, boat-shaped coffins and large stone coffins gave way to box-shaped wooden coffins. == Notable Tombs and Kofuns == '''Yayoi period''' * {{ill|Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja|市三宅東遺跡}} (Late Yayoi, Shiga Prefecture) * {{ill|Hirabaru Burial Site|ja|平原遺跡}} (Late Yayoi, Fukuoka Prefecture) '''Kofun period''' * {{ill|Eimoridzuka Kofun 2|ja|衛守塚古墳群}} (Early, Yamagata Prefecture) * {{ill|Tōmimizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳}} (Late Early, Miyagi Prefecture) * {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} (Early, Nara Prefecture) == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Kofun Dictionary|last=Ōtsuka|coauthors=Kobayashi|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|year=1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|title=The Birth of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Kondō|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|year=1986|isbn=4-00-010266-4}} * {{Cite book|title=The State of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Hirose|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=2003|isbn=4-04-703355-3}} == External Links == * [[File:Kuroduka kofun museum02s3872.jpg|200px|right|thumb|{{ill|Cinnabar|ja|辰砂}}-coated {{ill|Waritakegata wooden coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan}} replica inside the stone chamber of the {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}}. Visible are the base of the coffin and the {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirror|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}}.]] '''Waritakegata Wooden Coffin''' (割竹形木棺, *Waritakegata Mokukan*) refers to a type of hollowed-out wooden coffin commonly found during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. == Overview == This type of coffin is made by splitting a log lengthwise, hollowing out its interior to create the coffin body and lid, and forming an elongated cylindrical shape. It was often placed inside a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber}}.
The waritakegata wooden coffin is believed to have been conceived as a solemn burial vessel for the chieftain class and privileged elites buried in ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}}.
In well-preserved examples, the interior is divided into three sections: the deceased is placed in the central section, with grave goods arranged in the head and foot sections. The coffins were often placed on clay beds, gravel layers, or in chambers equipped with drainage systems, indicating meticulous construction of the burial facilities. Red pigments such as {{ill|Bengala|lt=red ochre|ja|ベンガラ}} (iron(III) oxide, Fe₂O₃) or cinnabar (mercury(II) sulfide, HgS) were sometimes applied to the interior and exterior of the coffin or the walls of the stone chamber. These acts are considered to have had ritual significance.Yoshitaka Hōjō, "Pit-style stone chambers and burial rituals," in {{ill|Makoto Sahara|ja|佐原真}}, 2007, pp. 451–453. == Construction and Materials == The coffins are made by hollowing out giant trees 5 to 8 meters in length and approximately 1 meter in diameter, resulting in the "waritakegata" (split-bamboo) shape. Both ends of the cylindrical coffin were sealed with wooden boards.
In addition to the waritakegata type, there were {{ill|boat-shaped wooden coffins|ja|舟形木棺}}, named for their resemblance to boat prows. These are less commonly found. Most of the source wood for the waritakegata coffins, especially west of the {{ill|Kinki region|ja|近畿地方}}, was evergreen conifers such as {{ill|Sciadopitys verticillata|lt=Kōyamaki|ja|コウヤマキ}}, whereas regions east of {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国}} used {{ill|cedar|ja|スギ}}, {{ill|zelkova|ja|ケヤキ}}, and {{ill|hinoki cypress|ja|ヒノキ}}.The {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} contained a coffin made from {{ill|mulberry|ja|クワ}} wood, suggesting the interred may have been connected to sericulture or possibly a female ruler.Examples include cedar from the Ichimiyake Higashi Site (Shiga Prefecture), hinoki from Hitachi Kagami Kofun (Ibaraki Prefecture), and zelkova from Eimoridzuka Kofun 2 (Yamagata Prefecture). Few coffins survive intact due to decay, and many structural details remain unknown.The shape of the coffin's bottom is often preserved as an arc-shaped trace on the coffin bed, even when the coffin itself has decayed. == Timeframe and Scale == Waritakegata coffins appeared in the early Kofun period, sometimes even in late {{ill|Yayoi tumulus burials|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs}}. While originally placed inside pit-style stone chambers or stone cists, later periods saw coffins encased in clay or gravel ({{ill|clay coffins|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}}, gravel cists) or buried directly into grave pits. {{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄}} established the connection between waritakegata coffins and burial facilities, and they are considered a defining feature of standardized early kofun burials.Morishta (2005), pp. 55–56. {{ill|Yoshirō Kondō|ja|近藤義郎}} proposed that {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} served as ritual sites for the succession of chieftain spirits, highlighting: # A significant leap in mound shape and scale. # The presence of waritakegata coffins and pit-style stone chambers. # Grave goods, including numerous {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror}}, following specific norms.Kondō (1986). {{ill|Kazuo Hirose|ja|広瀬和雄}} emphasized the unique features of early kofun, noting, for instance, the pairing of waritakegata coffins with pit-style stone cists in the {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} and {{ill|Moto Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳}}, compared to {{ill|Akatuka Kofun|ja|赤塚古墳}} in Oita, which used box-shaped stone coffins.Hirose (2003), pp. 155–156. The size of the burial chamber often correlated with the size of the coffin.Ōtsuka and Kobayashi, "Kofun Dictionary," 1982, pp. 367–368. Early examples measured 5–8 meters in length but became shorter over time, with late-period coffins averaging 2–3 meters. By the late Kofun period, boat-shaped coffins and large stone coffins gave way to box-shaped wooden coffins. == Notable Tombs and Kofuns == '''Yayoi period''' * {{ill|Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja|市三宅東遺跡}} (Late Yayoi, Shiga Prefecture) * {{ill|Hirabaru Burial Site|ja|平原遺跡}} (Late Yayoi, Fukuoka Prefecture) '''Kofun period''' * {{ill|Eimoridzuka Kofun 2|ja|衛守塚古墳群}} (Early, Yamagata Prefecture) * {{ill|Tōmimizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳}} (Late Early, Miyagi Prefecture) * {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳}} (Early, Nara Prefecture) == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Kofun
