revision.comment: 34 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:割竹形木棺 up to rev 103099329
revision.text:
[[ファイル:Kuroduka kofun museum02s3872.jpg|200px|right|thumb|[[辰砂|水銀朱]]で赤く塗られた[[黒塚古墳]]石室内の割竹形木棺（レプリカ）。
木棺底部と[[三角縁神獣鏡]]がみえる]] '''割竹形木棺'''（わりたけがたもくかん、わりたけがたもっかん）とは、[[古墳時代]]に多くみられる刳抜（くりぬき）式の[[木棺]]の一種である。 == 概要 == [[丸太]]を縦に二つ割りにして中を刳り抜き、棺身と棺蓋を造り合わせて長大な[[円筒]]形の[[棺]]としたもので、多くの場合は[[竪穴式石室]]に納められる。
この木棺は、[[古墳]]に埋葬される首長層や特権階級の人々のために造られた荘重な棺として考案されたと推測されている。
良好な遺存例では、内部は三分割され、遺骸は中央区画に、頭上側と足下側の区画に[[副葬品]]が納められていることがある。木棺を置く場所には粘土を敷く事例や墓壙底一面に砂利を敷く事例、排水施設を設けている事例など、石室は相当丁寧に構築されたのが分かる。木棺の内と外、石室の壁面に、しばしば赤色顔料（[[ベンガラ]]、酸化第二鉄Fe2O3）、棺の内側に[[辰砂|水銀朱]]（[[硫化水銀]]HgS）が塗られていることがある。このような構築行為が儀礼行為の側面を持っていたと考えられる。北條芳隆「竪穴式石室と埋葬儀礼」/佐原真(2007) 451－453ページ == 製法と材質 == 長さは5[[メートル|m]]～8m、直径1mもの巨木を刳り抜き、中に[[遺体]]を納める空洞部分を造ったことから「割竹形」の名がある。多くの場合、筒状の木棺の前後両端に板材を当てて閉鎖する方法を採用している。
刳抜式木棺としては、「割竹形」のほか「[[舟形木棺]]」があり、「舟形」は、棺の下半身の棺身底部を[[船]]の[[舳先]]のように削り出すところからその名があるが、検出例は少ない。いずれも、材となる原木は[[近畿地方]]以西では[[マツ目]]のなかでも[[スギ科]]に近い[[常緑樹]]、[[コウヤマキ]]が圧倒的に多い黒塚古墳では、[[クワ|桑]]（クワ）の木を刳り抜いて木棺を造っていた。このことから、被葬者は[[養蚕]]を把握した人物、あるいは、[[女性]]の権力者とする見方がある。が、[[近江国|近江]]以東では、[[スギ]]、[[ケヤキ]]、[[ヒノキ]]などもみられる市三宅東遺跡（滋賀県）ではスギ、常陸鏡塚古墳（茨城県）ではヒノキ、衛守塚2号墳（山形県）ではケヤキの割竹形木棺を確認している。舟形木棺の例では、下野七廻り鏡塚古墳（栃木県）の棺材がヒノキであった。。なお、木棺は、完全に遺存する例は少なく、腐朽してしまうことが多いので、細部構造に関しては不明な点が多い棺身そのものが遺存しなくても、棺身底部の横断面外形が棺床で上に開いた弧形の痕跡として残ることが多い。。 == 時期と規模 == 古墳時代前期、とくに出現期からみられ、さらに[[弥生墳丘墓]]からも検出されることがある。本来は[[竪穴式石室]]もしくは竪穴式石槨に納めたものと考えられるが、古墳時代中期中葉以降、[[粘土]]や[[礫]]で包んだり（[[粘土槨]]・礫槨）、木棺を[[直葬]]（直接、墓壙に埋葬）する場合もあった。なお、竪穴式石室が割竹形木棺を内包する埋葬施設であったことを解明したのは[[小林行雄]]であり森下（2005）p.55-56、今日でも出現期古墳における「定型化」の一要素として割竹形木棺が掲げられることが多い寺沢（2000）p.289-290。 「[[墳丘墓]]」の概念を[[弥生時代]]に導入した[[近藤義郎]]は、[[前方後円墳]]について「首長霊継承儀礼の場」との見解を示し、それが今日の定説となっている広瀬（2003）p.92-93、原出典は近藤（1983）。近藤は、弥生墳丘墓と前方後円墳との相違点として、 # 墳形・墳丘規模において「飛躍」がみられること # 埋葬構造として長大な割竹形木棺と竪穴式石室を有すること # 一定の規範にもとづく副葬品において中国鏡、とくに多数の[[三角縁神獣鏡]]を伴うこと などを掲げ近藤（1986）、ここでも割竹形木棺が出現期古墳の特徴の1つとして重視されていることがわかる。 それに対し[[広瀬和雄]]は、[[黒塚古墳]]（[[奈良県]][[天理市]]）{{R|kuro}}や[[元稲荷古墳]]（[[京都府]][[向日市]]）などのような割竹形木棺と竪穴式石槨の組み合わせに対し、[[赤塚古墳]]（[[大分県]][[宇佐市]]）では箱形石棺をともなっているなど、出現期古墳における個々の古墳の特殊性について指摘している広瀬（2003）p.155-156。 古墳時代前期にあっては、むしろ石室の規模は割竹形木棺の規模によって左右される様相がみてとれる大塚・小林編『古墳辞典』（1982）p.367-368。副葬品は、多量の[[銅鏡]]をはじめ[[鉄|鉄製]]の[[武器]]、農工具など呪術的性格の濃いものが多い。
また、古墳時代前期にあっては、通常、全長が5～8mにもおよぶ長大な規模長大になった理由として、棺に多量の副葬品を入れたからだと解釈されてきたが、調査研究が進み、棺自体が丸木船を象った可能性が指摘されている。（北條芳隆「竪穴式石室と埋葬儀礼」/佐原真(2007) 451ページ）であったのに対し、年代が下るにしたがって短くなり、後期には2～3m程度のものが多くなる。なお、前期後半には割竹形、舟形、[[長持]]形など多様な石製の棺（石棺）が増え、後期になると刳抜式木棺は「舟形」も含めて減少し、木棺としては、かわって組み合わせ式の[[箱形木棺]]が増える。 == 割竹形木棺をともなう古墳・墳丘墓 == '''弥生時代''' *[[市三宅東遺跡]]（弥生後期、[[方形周溝墓]][[滋賀県]][[野洲市]]） *[[平原遺跡|平原1号墓]]（弥生後期、方形周溝墓、[[福岡県]][[糸島市]]） '''古墳時代''' *[[衛守塚古墳群|衛守塚2号墳]]（後期初頭、[[山形県]][[天童市]]） *[[遠見塚古墳]]（前期末、前方後円墳、[[宮城県]][[仙台市]]） *[[会津大塚山古墳]]（前期、前方後円墳、[[福島県]][[会津若松市]]） *[[大安場古墳群|大安場1号墳]]（前期後半、[[前方後方墳]]、福島県[[郡山市]]） *[[桜井古墳]]（前期、前方後方墳、福島県[[南相馬市]]） *[[常陸鏡塚古墳]]（別名日下ヶ塚古墳、前期、前方後円墳、[[茨城県]][[大洗町]]） *[[伊屋之免古墳]]（前期、円墳、神奈川県川崎市） *[[甲斐銚子塚古墳]] （前期、前方後円墳、[[山梨県]][[甲府市]]） *[[三ツ山古墳群|三ツ山古墳1号墳]]・2号墳（前期、いずれも[[方墳]]、[[愛知県]][[小牧市]]） *[[椿井大塚山古墳]]（前期、前方後円墳、京都府[[木津川市]]） *[[黒塚古墳]]（前期初頭、前方後円墳、奈良県天理市） *[[中山大塚古墳]]（前期初頭、前方後円墳、奈良県天理市） *[[下池山古墳]]（前期、前方後方墳、奈良県天理市） *[[ホケノ山古墳]]（前期初頭、前方後円墳、奈良県[[桜井市]]） *[[桜塚古墳群 (豊中市)#小石塚古墳|小石塚古墳]]（前期末、前方後円墳、[[大阪府]][[豊中市]]） *[[桜塚古墳群 (豊中市)#大塚古墳|大塚古墳]]（[[円墳]]、大阪府豊中市） *[[桜塚古墳群 (豊中市)#南天平塚古墳|南天平塚古墳]]（前方後円墳（帆立貝式）、大阪府豊中市） *[[闘鶏山古墳]]（前期、前方後円墳、大阪府[[高槻市]]） *[[久宝寺1号墳]]（前期、方墳、大阪府[[八尾市]]） *[[誉田御廟山古墳#陪墳|誉田御廟山古墳盾塚]]（中期、誉田御廟山古墳[[陪塚]]、大阪府[[羽曳野市]]） *[[西求女塚古墳]]（前期、前方後方墳、[[兵庫県]][[神戸市]]） *[[那珂八幡古墳]]（前期初頭、前方後円墳、[[福岡県]][[福岡市]]） *[[川部・高森古墳群#免ヶ平古墳|免ヶ平古墳]]（前期、前方後円墳、大分県宇佐市） == 脚注 == == 出典 == * [[大塚初重]]・[[小林三郎 (考古学者)|小林三郎]]『古墳辞典』[[東京堂出版]]、1982年12月。ISBN 4-490-10165-1 * [[近藤義郎]]「前方後円墳の誕生」『岩波講座 日本考古学第6巻』[[岩波書店]]、1986年1月。ISBN 4-00-010266-4 * [[寺沢薫]]『日本の歴史02 王権誕生』[[講談社]]、2000年12月。ISBN 4-06-268902-2 * [[広瀬和雄]]『前方後円墳国家』[[角川書店]]<角川選書>、2003年。ISBN 4-04-703355-3 * [[森下章司]]「2 古墳の出現に関する議論」[[金関恕]]・[[山尾幸久]]・森下ほか『古墳のはじまりを考える』[[学生社]]、2005年5月。ISBN 4-311-20280-6 == 参考文献 == *近藤義郎『前方後円墳の時代』岩波書店<日本歴史叢書>、1983年4月。{{ASIN|B000J7ESLG}} *佐原真・ウエルナー=シュタインハウス監修　奈良文化財研究所編集『ドイツ展記念概説　日本の考古学（普及版）下巻』学生社　2007年4月 ISBN 978-4-311-75038-0 == 関連項目 == *[[古墳]] *[[棺]] *[[竪穴式石室]] *[[古墳時代]] *[[弥生墳丘墓]] *[[纒向型前方後円墳]] == 外部リンク == *[http://www.city.takatsuki.osaka.jp/rekishi/tugeyama_h18.html 史跡闘鶏山古墳石槨内画像調査について]（[[高槻市]]インターネット歴史館）／[[闘鶏山古墳]]の割竹形木棺 *[http://www.occh.or.jp/kako_news/sinpojiumu_kawatiheiya.html 河内平野における古墳の出現－久宝寺遺跡と加美遺跡－]（[[大阪歴史博物館]]）／久宝寺1号墳の割竹形木棺 *{{Wayback|url=http://sankei.jp.msn.com/culture/academic/090330/acd0903302033002-n1.htm |title=最古の割竹形木棺か? 滋賀県･市三宅東遺跡 |date=20090402234400}}（MSN産経ニュース、2009年3月30日） *[http://www.city.yasu.lg.jp/doc/seisakusuisinbu/kouhouhishoka/kouhouhishoka/files/5955.doc 市三宅東遺跡出土割竹形木棺状木製品記者発表資料]（野洲市教育委員会、docファイル形式） [[ファイル:Kuroduka kofun museum02s3872.jpg|200px|right|thumb|[[辰砂|水銀朱]]で赤く塗られた[[黒塚古墳]]石室内の割竹形木棺（レプリカ）。
木棺底部と[[三角縁神獣鏡]]がみえる]] '''割竹形木棺'''（わりたけがたもくかん、わりたけがたもっかん）とは、[[古墳時代]]に多くみられる刳抜（くりぬき）式の[[木棺]]の一種である。 == 概要 == [[丸太]]を縦に二つ割りにして中を刳り抜き、棺身と棺蓋を造り合わせて長大な[[円筒]]形の[[棺]]としたもので、多くの場合は[[竪穴式石室]]に納められる。
この木棺は、[[古墳]]に埋葬される首長層や特権階級の人々のために造られた荘重な棺として考案されたと推測されている。
良好な遺存例では、内部は三分割され、遺骸は中央区画に、頭上側と足下側の区画に[[副葬品]]が納められていることがある。木棺を置く場所には粘土を敷く事例や墓壙底一面に砂利を敷く事例、排水施設を設けている事例など、石室は相当丁寧に構築されたのが分かる。木棺の内と外、石室の壁面に、しばしば赤色顔料（[[ベンガラ]]、酸化第二鉄Fe2O3）、棺の内側に[[辰砂|水銀朱]]（[[硫化水銀]]HgS）が塗られていることがある。このような構築行為が儀礼行為の側面を持っていたと考えられる。北條芳隆「竪穴式石室と埋葬儀礼」/佐原真(2007) 451－453ページ == 製法と材質 == 長さは5[[メートル|m]]～8m、直径1mもの巨木を刳り抜き、中に[[遺体]]を納める空洞部分を造ったことから「割竹形」の名がある。多くの場合、筒状の木棺の前後両端に板材を当てて閉鎖する方法を採用している。
刳抜式木棺としては、「割竹形」のほか「[[舟形木棺]]」があり、「舟形」は、棺の下半身の棺身底部を[[船]]の[[舳先]]のように削り出すところからその名があるが、検出例は少ない。いずれも、材となる原木は[[近畿地方]]以西では[[マツ目]]のなかでも[[スギ科]]に近い[[常緑樹]]、[[コウヤマキ]]が圧倒的に多い黒塚古墳では、[[クワ|桑]]（クワ）の木を刳り抜いて木棺を造っていた。このことから、被葬者は[[養蚕]]を把握した人物、あるいは、[[女性]]の権力者とする見方がある。が、[[近江国|近江]]以東では、[[スギ]]、[[ケヤキ]]、[[ヒノキ]]などもみられる市三宅東遺跡（滋賀県）ではスギ、常陸鏡塚古墳（茨城県）ではヒノキ、衛守塚2号墳（山形県）ではケヤキの割竹形木棺を確認している。舟形木棺の例では、下野七廻り鏡塚古墳（栃木県）の棺材がヒノキであった。。なお、木棺は、完全に遺存する例は少なく、腐朽してしまうことが多いので、細部構造に関しては不明な点が多い棺身そのものが遺存しなくても、棺身底部の横断面外形が棺床で上に開いた弧形の痕跡として残ることが多い。。 == 時期と規模 == 古墳時代前期、とくに出現期からみられ、さらに[[弥生墳丘墓]]からも検出されることがある。本来は[[竪穴式石室]]もしくは竪穴式石槨に納めたものと考えられるが、古墳時代中期中葉以降、[[粘土]]や[[礫]]で包んだり（[[粘土槨]]・礫槨）、木棺を[[直葬]]（直接、墓壙に埋葬）する場合もあった。なお、竪穴式石室が割竹形木棺を内包する埋葬施設であったことを解明したのは[[小林行雄]]であり森下（2005）p.55-56、今日でも出現期古墳における「定型化」の一要素として割竹形木棺が掲げられることが多い寺沢（2000）p.289-290。 「[[墳丘墓]]」の概念を[[弥生時代]]に導入した[[近藤義郎]]は、[[前方後円墳]]について「首長霊継承儀礼の場」との見解を示し、それが今日の定説となっている広瀬（2003）p.92-93、原出典は近藤（1983）。近藤は、弥生墳丘墓と前方後円墳との相違点として、 # 墳形・墳丘規模において「飛躍」がみられること # 埋葬構造として長大な割竹形木棺と竪穴式石室を有すること # 一定の規範にもとづく副葬品において中国鏡、とくに多数の[[三角縁神獣鏡]]を伴うこと などを掲げ近藤（1986）、ここでも割竹形木棺が出現期古墳の特徴の1つとして重視されていることがわかる。 それに対し[[広瀬和雄]]は、[[黒塚古墳]]（[[奈良県]][[天理市]]）{{R|kuro}}や[[元稲荷古墳]]（[[京都府]][[向日市]]）などのような割竹形木棺と竪穴式石槨の組み合わせに対し、[[赤塚古墳]]（[[大分県]][[宇佐市]]）では箱形石棺をともなっているなど、出現期古墳における個々の古墳の特殊性について指摘している広瀬（2003）p.155-156。 古墳時代前期にあっては、むしろ石室の規模は割竹形木棺の規模によって左右される様相がみてとれる大塚・小林編『古墳辞典』（1982）p.367-368。副葬品は、多量の[[銅鏡]]をはじめ[[鉄|鉄製]]の[[武器]]、農工具など呪術的性格の濃いものが多い。
また、古墳時代前期にあっては、通常、全長が5～8mにもおよぶ長大な規模長大になった理由として、棺に多量の副葬品を入れたからだと解釈されてきたが、調査研究が進み、棺自体が丸木船を象った可能性が指摘されている。（北條芳隆「竪穴式石室と埋葬儀礼」/佐原真(2007) 451ページ）であったのに対し、年代が下るにしたがって短くなり、後期には2～3m程度のものが多くなる。なお、前期後半には割竹形、舟形、[[長持]]形など多様な石製の棺（石棺）が増え、後期になると刳抜式木棺は「舟形」も含めて減少し、木棺としては、かわって組み合わせ式の[[箱形木棺]]が増える。 == 割竹形木棺をともなう古墳・墳丘墓 == '''弥生時代''' {{afc comment|1=Need to find a consistent nomenclature for all of these. Sekkan and Mokka need some level of consistency because of how they are contrasted here * [[Funagata Mokkan]] * [[Funagata Sekkan]] * [[House-shaped Sarcophagus]] * [[Box-shaped stone coffin]] * [[Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus]] * [[Sekkan]] * [[Waritakegata Mokkan]] * [[Waritakegata Sekkan]] * [[Wooden Coffin]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:43, 28 April 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Kuroduka kofun museum02s3872.jpg|200px|right|thumb|{{ill|Cinnabar|ja|辰砂|qq=draft|qq=draft}}-coated {{ill|Waritakegata wooden coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}} replica inside the stone chamber of the {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Visible are the base of the coffin and the {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirror|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.]] '''Waritakegata Wooden Coffin''' (割竹形木棺, *Waritakegata Mokukan*) refers to a type of hollowed-out wooden coffin commonly found during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Overview == This type of coffin is made by splitting a log lengthwise, hollowing out its interior to create the coffin body and lid, and forming an elongated cylindrical shape. It was often placed inside a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=竪穴式石室|4=zh|5=豎穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
The waritakegata wooden coffin is believed to have been conceived as a solemn burial vessel for the chieftain class and privileged elites buried in ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
In well-preserved examples, the interior is divided into three sections: the deceased is placed in the central section, with grave goods arranged in the head and foot sections. The coffins were often placed on clay beds, gravel layers, or in chambers equipped with drainage systems, indicating meticulous construction of the burial facilities. Red pigments such as {{ill|Bengala|lt=red ochre|ja|ベンガラ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (iron(III) oxide, Fe₂O₃) or cinnabar (mercury(II) sulfide, HgS) were sometimes applied to the interior and exterior of the coffin or the walls of the stone chamber. These acts are considered to have had ritual significance.Yoshitaka Hōjō, "Pit-style stone chambers and burial rituals," in {{ill|Makoto Sahara|ja|佐原真|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, 2007, pp. 451–453. == Construction and Materials == The coffins are made by hollowing out giant trees 5 to 8 meters in length and approximately 1 meter in diameter, resulting in the "waritakegata" (split-bamboo) shape. Both ends of the cylindrical coffin were sealed with wooden boards.
In addition to the waritakegata type, there were {{ill|boat-shaped wooden coffins|ja|舟形木棺|1=Funagata Mokkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, named for their resemblance to boat prows. These are less commonly found. Most of the source wood for the waritakegata coffins, especially west of the {{ill|Kinki region|ja|近畿地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, was evergreen conifers such as {{ill|Sciadopitys verticillata|lt=Kōyamaki|ja|コウヤマキ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, whereas regions east of {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} used {{ill|cedar|ja|スギ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|zelkova|ja|ケヤキ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|hinoki cypress|ja|ヒノキ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.The {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} contained a coffin made from {{ill|mulberry|ja|クワ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} wood, suggesting the interred may have been connected to sericulture or possibly a female ruler.Examples include cedar from the Ichimiyake Higashi Site (Shiga Prefecture), hinoki from Hitachi Kagami Kofun (Ibaraki Prefecture), and zelkova from Eimoridzuka Kofun 2 (Yamagata Prefecture). Few coffins survive intact due to decay, and many structural details remain unknown.The shape of the coffin's bottom is often preserved as an arc-shaped trace on the coffin bed, even when the coffin itself has decayed. == Timeframe and Scale == Waritakegata coffins appeared in the early Kofun period, sometimes even in late {{ill|Yayoi tumulus burials|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. While originally placed inside pit-style stone chambers or stone cists, later periods saw coffins encased in clay or gravel ({{ill|clay coffins|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, gravel cists) or buried directly into grave pits. {{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄|qq=draft|qq=draft}} established the connection between waritakegata coffins and burial facilities, and they are considered a defining feature of standardized early kofun burials.Morishta (2005), pp. 55–56. {{ill|Yoshirō Kondō|ja|近藤義郎|qq=draft|qq=draft}} proposed that {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} served as ritual sites for the succession of chieftain spirits, highlighting: # A significant leap in mound shape and scale. # The presence of waritakegata coffins and pit-style stone chambers. # Grave goods, including numerous {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, following specific norms.Kondō (1986). {{ill|Kazuo Hirose|ja|広瀬和雄|qq=draft|qq=draft}} emphasized the unique features of early kofun, noting, for instance, the pairing of waritakegata coffins with pit-style stone cists in the {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Moto Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, compared to {{ill|Akatuka Kofun|ja|赤塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in Oita, which used box-shaped stone coffins.Hirose (2003), pp. 155–156. The size of the burial chamber often correlated with the size of the coffin.Ōtsuka and Kobayashi, "Kofun Dictionary," 1982, pp. 367–368. Early examples measured 5–8 meters in length but became shorter over time, with late-period coffins averaging 2–3 meters. By the late Kofun period, boat-shaped coffins and large stone coffins gave way to box-shaped wooden coffins. == Notable Tombs and Kofuns == '''Yayoi period''' * {{ill|Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja|市三宅東遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Late Yayoi, Shiga Prefecture) * {{ill|Hirabaru Burial Site|ja|平原遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Late Yayoi, Fukuoka Prefecture) '''Kofun period''' * {{ill|Eimoridzuka Kofun 2|ja|衛守塚古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Early, Yamagata Prefecture) * {{ill|Tōmimizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Late Early, Miyagi Prefecture) * {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Early, Nara Prefecture) == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Kofun Dictionary|last=Ōtsuka|coauthors=Kobayashi|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|year=1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|title=The Birth of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Kondō|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|year=1986|isbn=4-00-010266-4}} * {{Cite book|title=The State of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Hirose|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=2003|isbn=4-04-703355-3}} == External Links == * [http://example.com Example external link] {{draft categories| }} {{citation needed|Split-bamboo coffin}} {{Kofun navbar}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:Japanese archaeological artifacts]] [[Category:Archaeological features]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:Coffins]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Waritakegata Sekkan]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Waritakegata Mokkan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Coffins to comment]] [[Category:Coffins to comment]][[sn:Waritakegata Mokkan]][[Category:Coffins to comment]] [[Category:Coffins to comment]][[sn:Waritakegata Mokkan]][[Category:Coffins to comment]] }} [[sn:Waritakegata Mokkan]] [[ja:割竹形木棺]] {{translated page|ja|割竹形木棺|version=103099329|comment=Imported full ja history}}

{{afc comment|1=Need to find a consistent nomenclature for all of these. Sekkan and Mokka need some level of consistency because of how they are contrasted here * [[Funagata Mokkan]] * [[Funagata Sekkan]] * [[House-shaped Sarcophagus]] * [[Box-shaped stone coffin]] * [[Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus]] * [[Sekkan]] * [[Waritakegata Mokkan]] * [[Waritakegata Sekkan]] * [[Wooden Coffin]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:43, 28 April 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Kuroduka kofun museum02s3872.jpg|200px|right|thumb|{{ill|Cinnabar|ja|辰砂|qq=draft|qq=draft}}-coated {{ill|Waritakegata wooden coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}} replica inside the stone chamber of the {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Visible are the base of the coffin and the {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirror|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.]] '''Waritakegata Wooden Coffin''' (割竹形木棺, *Waritakegata Mokukan*) refers to a type of hollowed-out wooden coffin commonly found during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. == Overview == This type of coffin is made by splitting a log lengthwise, hollowing out its interior to create the coffin body and lid, and forming an elongated cylindrical shape. It was often placed inside a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=竪穴式石室|4=zh|5=豎穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
The waritakegata wooden coffin is believed to have been conceived as a solemn burial vessel for the chieftain class and privileged elites buried in ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
In well-preserved examples, the interior is divided into three sections: the deceased is placed in the central section, with grave goods arranged in the head and foot sections. The coffins were often placed on clay beds, gravel layers, or in chambers equipped with drainage systems, indicating meticulous construction of the burial facilities. Red pigments such as {{ill|Bengala|lt=red ochre|ja|ベンガラ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (iron(III) oxide, Fe₂O₃) or cinnabar (mercury(II) sulfide, HgS) were sometimes applied to the interior and exterior of the coffin or the walls of the stone chamber. These acts are considered to have had ritual significance.Yoshitaka Hōjō, "Pit-style stone chambers and burial rituals," in {{ill|Makoto Sahara|ja|佐原真|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, 2007, pp. 451–453. == Construction and Materials == The coffins are made by hollowing out giant trees 5 to 8 meters in length and approximately 1 meter in diameter, resulting in the "waritakegata" (split-bamboo) shape. Both ends of the cylindrical coffin were sealed with wooden boards.
In addition to the waritakegata type, there were {{ill|boat-shaped wooden coffins|ja|舟形木棺|1=Funagata Mokkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, named for their resemblance to boat prows. These are less commonly found. Most of the source wood for the waritakegata coffins, especially west of the {{ill|Kinki region|ja|近畿地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, was evergreen conifers such as {{ill|Sciadopitys verticillata|lt=Kōyamaki|ja|コウヤマキ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, whereas regions east of {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国|qq=draft|qq=draft}} used {{ill|cedar|ja|スギ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|zelkova|ja|ケヤキ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|hinoki cypress|ja|ヒノキ|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.The {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} contained a coffin made from {{ill|mulberry|ja|クワ|qq=draft|qq=draft}} wood, suggesting the interred may have been connected to sericulture or possibly a female ruler.Examples include cedar from the Ichimiyake Higashi Site (Shiga Prefecture), hinoki from Hitachi Kagami Kofun (Ibaraki Prefecture), and zelkova from Eimoridzuka Kofun 2 (Yamagata Prefecture). Few coffins survive intact due to decay, and many structural details remain unknown.The shape of the coffin's bottom is often preserved as an arc-shaped trace on the coffin bed, even when the coffin itself has decayed. == Timeframe and Scale == Waritakegata coffins appeared in the early Kofun period, sometimes even in late {{ill|Yayoi tumulus burials|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. While originally placed inside pit-style stone chambers or stone cists, later periods saw coffins encased in clay or gravel ({{ill|clay coffins|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, gravel cists) or buried directly into grave pits. {{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄|qq=draft|qq=draft}} established the connection between waritakegata coffins and burial facilities, and they are considered a defining feature of standardized early kofun burials.Morishta (2005), pp. 55–56. {{ill|Yoshirō Kondō|ja|近藤義郎|qq=draft|qq=draft}} proposed that {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft}} served as ritual sites for the succession of chieftain spirits, highlighting: # A significant leap in mound shape and scale. # The presence of waritakegata coffins and pit-style stone chambers. # Grave goods, including numerous {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, following specific norms.Kondō (1986). {{ill|Kazuo Hirose|ja|広瀬和雄|qq=draft|qq=draft}} emphasized the unique features of early kofun, noting, for instance, the pairing of waritakegata coffins with pit-style stone cists in the {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Moto Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, compared to {{ill|Akatuka Kofun|ja|赤塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in Oita, which used box-shaped stone coffins.Hirose (2003), pp. 155–156. The size of the burial chamber often correlated with the size of the coffin.Ōtsuka and Kobayashi, "Kofun Dictionary," 1982, pp. 367–368. Early examples measured 5–8 meters in length but became shorter over time, with late-period coffins averaging 2–3 meters. By the late Kofun period, boat-shaped coffins and large stone coffins gave way to box-shaped wooden coffins. == Notable Tombs and Kofuns == '''Yayoi period''' * {{ill|Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja|市三宅東遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Late Yayoi, Shiga Prefecture) * {{ill|Hirabaru Burial Site|ja|平原遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Late Yayoi, Fukuoka Prefecture) '''Kofun period''' * {{ill|Eimoridzuka Kofun 2|ja|衛守塚古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Early, Yamagata Prefecture) * {{ill|Tōmimizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Late Early, Miyagi Prefecture) * {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Early, Nara Prefecture) == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Kofun Dictionary|last=Ōtsuka|coauthors=Kobayashi|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|year=1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|title=The Birth of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Kondō|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|year=1986|isbn=4-00-010266-4}} * {{Cite book|title=The State of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Hirose|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=2003|isbn=4-04-703355-3}} == External Links == * [http://example.com Example external link] {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|割竹形木棺|version=103099329|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:Japanese archaeological artifacts]] [[Category:Archaeological features]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:Coffins]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Waritakegata Sekkan]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Waritakegata Mokkan]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Coffins to comment]] [[Category:Coffins to comment]][[sn:Waritakegata Mokkan]][[Category:Coffins to comment]] [[Category:Coffins to comment]][[sn:Waritakegata Mokkan]][[Category:Coffins to comment]] }} [[sn:Waritakegata Mokkan]] [[ja:割竹形木棺]]

revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=Need to find a consistent nomenclature for all of these. Sekkan and Mokka need some level of consistency because of how they are contrasted here * [[Funagata Mokkan]] * [[Funagata Sekkan]] * [[House-shaped Sarcophagus]] * [[Box-shaped stone coffin]] * [[Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus]] * [[Sekkan]] * [[Waritakegata Mokkan]] * [[Waritakegata Sekkan]] * [[Wooden Coffin]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:43, 28 April 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Kuroduka kofun museum02s3872.jpg|200px|right|thumb|{{ill|Cinnabar|ja|辰砂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cinnabar|qq=}}-coated {{ill|Waritakegata wooden coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Waritakegata wooden coffin|13=User:Immanuelle/Waritakegata Mokkan|qq=}} replica inside the stone chamber of the {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurotsuka Kofun|qq=}}. Visible are the base of the coffin and the {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirror|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirror|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=}}.]] '''Waritakegata Wooden Coffin''' (割竹形木棺, *Waritakegata Mokukan*) refers to a type of hollowed-out wooden coffin commonly found during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun period|qq=}}. == Overview == This type of coffin is made by splitting a log lengthwise, hollowing out its interior to create the coffin body and lid, and forming an elongated cylindrical shape. It was often placed inside a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=竪穴式石室|4=zh|5=豎穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pit-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}.
The waritakegata wooden coffin is believed to have been conceived as a solemn burial vessel for the chieftain class and privileged elites buried in ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun|qq=}}.
In well-preserved examples, the interior is divided into three sections: the deceased is placed in the central section, with grave goods arranged in the head and foot sections. The coffins were often placed on clay beds, gravel layers, or in chambers equipped with drainage systems, indicating meticulous construction of the burial facilities. Red pigments such as {{ill|Bengala|lt=red ochre|ja|ベンガラ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bengala|qq=}} (iron(III) oxide, Fe₂O₃) or cinnabar (mercury(II) sulfide, HgS) were sometimes applied to the interior and exterior of the coffin or the walls of the stone chamber. These acts are considered to have had ritual significance.Yoshitaka Hōjō, "Pit-style stone chambers and burial rituals," in {{ill|Makoto Sahara|ja|佐原真|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makoto Sahara|qq=}}, 2007, pp. 451–453. == Construction and Materials == The coffins are made by hollowing out giant trees 5 to 8 meters in length and approximately 1 meter in diameter, resulting in the "waritakegata" (split-bamboo) shape. Both ends of the cylindrical coffin were sealed with wooden boards.
In addition to the waritakegata type, there were {{ill|boat-shaped wooden coffins|ja|舟形木棺|1=Funagata Mokkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/boat-shaped wooden coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Funagata Mokkan|qq=}}, named for their resemblance to boat prows. These are less commonly found. Most of the source wood for the waritakegata coffins, especially west of the {{ill|Kinki region|ja|近畿地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinki region|qq=}}, was evergreen conifers such as {{ill|Sciadopitys verticillata|lt=Kōyamaki|ja|コウヤマキ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sciadopitys verticillata|qq=}}, whereas regions east of {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi Province|qq=}} used {{ill|cedar|ja|スギ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cedar|qq=}}, {{ill|zelkova|ja|ケヤキ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zelkova|qq=}}, and {{ill|hinoki cypress|ja|ヒノキ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hinoki cypress|qq=}}.The {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurotsuka Kofun|qq=}} contained a coffin made from {{ill|mulberry|ja|クワ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mulberry|qq=}} wood, suggesting the interred may have been connected to sericulture or possibly a female ruler.Examples include cedar from the Ichimiyake Higashi Site (Shiga Prefecture), hinoki from Hitachi Kagami Kofun (Ibaraki Prefecture), and zelkova from Eimoridzuka Kofun 2 (Yamagata Prefecture). Few coffins survive intact due to decay, and many structural details remain unknown.The shape of the coffin's bottom is often preserved as an arc-shaped trace on the coffin bed, even when the coffin itself has decayed. == Timeframe and Scale == Waritakegata coffins appeared in the early Kofun period, sometimes even in late {{ill|Yayoi tumulus burials|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi tumulus burials|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}}. While originally placed inside pit-style stone chambers or stone cists, later periods saw coffins encased in clay or gravel ({{ill|clay coffins|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clay coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}}, gravel cists) or buried directly into grave pits. {{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobayashi Yukio|qq=}} established the connection between waritakegata coffins and burial facilities, and they are considered a defining feature of standardized early kofun burials.Morishta (2005), pp. 55–56. {{ill|Yoshirō Kondō|ja|近藤義郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshirō Kondō|qq=}} proposed that {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/keyhole-shaped tumuli|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|qq=}} served as ritual sites for the succession of chieftain spirits, highlighting: # A significant leap in mound shape and scale. # The presence of waritakegata coffins and pit-style stone chambers. # Grave goods, including numerous {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=}}, following specific norms.Kondō (1986). {{ill|Kazuo Hirose|ja|広瀬和雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazuo Hirose|qq=}} emphasized the unique features of early kofun, noting, for instance, the pairing of waritakegata coffins with pit-style stone cists in the {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurotsuka Kofun|qq=}} and {{ill|Moto Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto Inari Kofun|qq=}}, compared to {{ill|Akatuka Kofun|ja|赤塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akatuka Kofun|qq=}} in Oita, which used box-shaped stone coffins.Hirose (2003), pp. 155–156. The size of the burial chamber often correlated with the size of the coffin.Ōtsuka and Kobayashi, "Kofun Dictionary," 1982, pp. 367–368. Early examples measured 5–8 meters in length but became shorter over time, with late-period coffins averaging 2–3 meters. By the late Kofun period, boat-shaped coffins and large stone coffins gave way to box-shaped wooden coffins. == Notable Tombs and Kofuns == '''Yayoi period''' * {{ill|Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja|市三宅東遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichimiyake Higashi Site|qq=}} (Late Yayoi, Shiga Prefecture) * {{ill|Hirabaru Burial Site|ja|平原遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirabaru Burial Site|qq=}} (Late Yayoi, Fukuoka Prefecture) '''Kofun period''' * {{ill|Eimoridzuka Kofun 2|ja|衛守塚古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eimoridzuka Kofun 2|qq=}} (Early, Yamagata Prefecture) * {{ill|Tōmimizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōmimizuka Kofun|qq=}} (Late Early, Miyagi Prefecture) * {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurotsuka Kofun|qq=}} (Early, Nara Prefecture) == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Kofun Dictionary|last=Ōtsuka|coauthors=Kobayashi|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|year=1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|title=The Birth of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Kondō|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|year=1986|isbn=4-00-010266-4}} * {{Cite book|title=The State of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Hirose|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=2003|isbn=4-04-703355-3}} == External Links == * [http://example.com Example external link] {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|割竹形木棺|version=103099329|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Kofun template and pages that transclude it 2]] [[Category:Japanese archaeological artifacts]] [[Category:Archaeological features]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:Coffins]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Waritakegata Sekkan]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[sn:Waritakegata Mokkan]][[Category:Coffins to comment]] [[Category:Coffins to comment]][[sn:Waritakegata Mokkan]][[Category:Coffins to comment]] [[Category:Coffins to comment]][[sn:Waritakegata Mokkan]][[Category:Coffins to comment]] }} [[sn:Waritakegata Mokkan]] [[ja:割竹形木棺]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|前方後円墳|" to "|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|"

In well-preserved examples, the interior is divided into three sections: the deceased is placed in the central section, with grave goods arranged in the head and foot sections. The coffins were often placed on clay beds, gravel layers, or in chambers equipped with drainage systems, indicating meticulous construction of the burial facilities. Red pigments such as {{ill|Bengala|lt=red ochre|ja|ベンガラ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bengala|qq=}} (iron(III) oxide, Fe₂O₃) or cinnabar (mercury(II) sulfide, HgS) were sometimes applied to the interior and exterior of the coffin or the walls of the stone chamber. These acts are considered to have had ritual significance.Yoshitaka Hōjō, "Pit-style stone chambers and burial rituals," in {{ill|Makoto Sahara|ja|佐原真|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makoto Sahara|qq=}}, 2007, pp. 451–453. == Construction and Materials == The coffins are made by hollowing out giant trees 5 to 8 meters in length and approximately 1 meter in diameter, resulting in the "waritakegata" (split-bamboo) shape. Both ends of the cylindrical coffin were sealed with wooden boards.
In addition to the waritakegata type, there were {{ill|boat-shaped wooden coffins|ja|舟形木棺|1=Funagata Mokkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/boat-shaped wooden coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Funagata Mokkan|qq=}}, named for their resemblance to boat prows. These are less commonly found. Most of the source wood for the waritakegata coffins, especially west of the {{ill|Kinki region|ja|近畿地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinki region|qq=}}, was evergreen conifers such as {{ill|Sciadopitys verticillata|lt=Kōyamaki|ja|コウヤマキ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sciadopitys verticillata|qq=}}, whereas regions east of {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi Province|qq=}} used {{ill|cedar|ja|スギ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cedar|qq=}}, {{ill|zelkova|ja|ケヤキ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zelkova|qq=}}, and {{ill|hinoki cypress|ja|ヒノキ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hinoki cypress|qq=}}.The {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurotsuka Kofun|qq=}} contained a coffin made from {{ill|mulberry|ja|クワ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mulberry|qq=}} wood, suggesting the interred may have been connected to sericulture or possibly a female ruler.Examples include cedar from the Ichimiyake Higashi Site (Shiga Prefecture), hinoki from Hitachi Kagami Kofun (Ibaraki Prefecture), and zelkova from Eimoridzuka Kofun 2 (Yamagata Prefecture). Few coffins survive intact due to decay, and many structural details remain unknown.The shape of the coffin's bottom is often preserved as an arc-shaped trace on the coffin bed, even when the coffin itself has decayed. == Timeframe and Scale == Waritakegata coffins appeared in the early Kofun period, sometimes even in late {{ill|Yayoi tumulus burials|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi tumulus burials|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}}. While originally placed inside pit-style stone chambers or stone cists, later periods saw coffins encased in clay or gravel ({{ill|clay coffins|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clay coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}}, gravel cists) or buried directly into grave pits. {{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobayashi Yukio|qq=}} established the connection between waritakegata coffins and burial facilities, and they are considered a defining feature of standardized early kofun burials.Morishta (2005), pp. 55–56. {{ill|Yoshirō Kondō|ja|近藤義郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshirō Kondō|qq=}} proposed that {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/keyhole-shaped tumuli|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|qq=}} served as ritual sites for the succession of chieftain spirits, highlighting: # A significant leap in mound shape and scale. # The presence of waritakegata coffins and pit-style stone chambers. # Grave goods, including numerous {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=}}, following specific norms.Kondō (1986). {{ill|Kazuo Hirose|ja|広瀬和雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazuo Hirose|qq=}} emphasized the unique features of early kofun, noting, for instance, the pairing of waritakegata coffins with pit-style stone cists in the {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurotsuka Kofun|qq=}} and {{ill|Moto Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto Inari Kofun|qq=}}, compared to {{ill|Akatuka Kofun|ja|赤塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akatuka Kofun|qq=}} in Oita, which used box-shaped stone coffins.Hirose (2003), pp. 155–156. The size of the burial chamber often correlated with the size of the coffin.Ōtsuka and Kobayashi, "Kofun Dictionary," 1982, pp. 367–368. Early examples measured 5–8 meters in length but became shorter over time, with late-period coffins averaging 2–3 meters. By the late Kofun period, boat-shaped coffins and large stone coffins gave way to box-shaped wooden coffins. == Notable Tombs and Kofuns == '''Yayoi period''' * {{ill|Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja|市三宅東遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichimiyake Higashi Site|qq=}} (Late Yayoi, Shiga Prefecture) * {{ill|Hirabaru Ruins|ja|平原遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirabaru Burial Site|qq=}} (Late Yayoi, Fukuoka Prefecture) '''Kofun period''' * {{ill|Eimoridzuka Kofun 2|ja|衛守塚古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eimoridzuka Kofun 2|qq=}} (Early, Yamagata Prefecture) * {{ill|Tōmimizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōmimizuka Kofun|qq=}} (Late Early, Miyagi Prefecture) * {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurotsuka Kofun|qq=}} (Early, Nara Prefecture) == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Kofun Dictionary|last=Ōtsuka|coauthors=Kobayashi|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|year=1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|title=The Birth of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Kondō|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|year=1986|isbn=4-00-010266-4}} * {{Cite book|title=The State of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Hirose|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=2003|isbn=4-04-703355-3}} == External Links == * Sekkan]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[sn:Waritakegata Mokkan]][[Category:Coffins to comment]] [[Category:Coffins to comment]][[sn:Waritakegata Mokkan]][[Category:Coffins to comment]] {{afc comment|1=Need to find a consistent nomenclature for all of these. Sekkan and Mokka need some level of consistency because of how they are contrasted here * [[Funagata Mokkan]] * [[Funagata Sekkan]] * [[House-shaped Sarcophagus]] * [[Box-shaped stone coffin]] * [[Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus]] * [[Sekkan]] * [[Waritakegata Mokkan]] * [[Waritakegata Sekkan]] * [[Wooden Coffin]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:43, 28 April 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Kuroduka kofun museum02s3872.jpg|200px|right|thumb|{{ill|Cinnabar|ja|辰砂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cinnabar|qq=}}-coated {{ill|Waritakegata wooden coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Waritakegata wooden coffin|13=User:Immanuelle/Waritakegata Mokkan|qq=}} replica inside the stone chamber of the {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurotsuka Kofun|qq=}}. Visible are the base of the coffin and the {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirror|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirror|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=}}.]] '''Waritakegata Wooden Coffin''' (割竹形木棺, *Waritakegata Mokukan*) refers to a type of hollowed-out wooden coffin commonly found during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun period|qq=}}. == Overview == This type of coffin is made by splitting a log lengthwise, hollowing out its interior to create the coffin body and lid, and forming an elongated cylindrical shape. It was often placed inside a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=竪穴式石室|4=zh|5=豎穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pit-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}.
The waritakegata wooden coffin is believed to have been conceived as a solemn burial vessel for the chieftain class and privileged elites buried in ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun|qq=}}.
In well-preserved examples, the interior is divided into three sections: the deceased is placed in the central section, with grave goods arranged in the head and foot sections. The coffins were often placed on clay beds, gravel layers, or in chambers equipped with drainage systems, indicating meticulous construction of the burial facilities. Red pigments such as {{ill|Bengala|lt=red ochre|ja|ベンガラ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bengala|qq=}} (iron(III) oxide, Fe₂O₃) or cinnabar (mercury(II) sulfide, HgS) were sometimes applied to the interior and exterior of the coffin or the walls of the stone chamber. These acts are considered to have had ritual significance.Yoshitaka Hōjō, "Pit-style stone chambers and burial rituals," in {{ill|Makoto Sahara|ja|佐原真|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makoto Sahara|qq=}}, 2007, pp. 451–453. == Construction and Materials == The coffins are made by hollowing out giant trees 5 to 8 meters in length and approximately 1 meter in diameter, resulting in the "waritakegata" (split-bamboo) shape. Both ends of the cylindrical coffin were sealed with wooden boards.
In addition to the waritakegata type, there were {{ill|boat-shaped wooden coffins|ja|舟形木棺|1=Funagata Mokkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/boat-shaped wooden coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Funagata Mokkan|qq=}}, named for their resemblance to boat prows. These are less commonly found. Most of the source wood for the waritakegata coffins, especially west of the {{ill|Kinki region|ja|近畿地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinki region|qq=}}, was evergreen conifers such as {{ill|Sciadopitys verticillata|lt=Kōyamaki|ja|コウヤマキ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sciadopitys verticillata|qq=}}, whereas regions east of {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi Province|qq=}} used {{ill|cedar|ja|スギ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cedar|qq=}}, {{ill|zelkova|ja|ケヤキ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zelkova|qq=}}, and {{ill|hinoki cypress|ja|ヒノキ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hinoki cypress|qq=}}.The {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurotsuka Kofun|qq=}} contained a coffin made from {{ill|mulberry|ja|クワ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mulberry|qq=}} wood, suggesting the interred may have been connected to sericulture or possibly a female ruler.Examples include cedar from the Ichimiyake Higashi Site (Shiga Prefecture), hinoki from Hitachi Kagami Kofun (Ibaraki Prefecture), and zelkova from Eimoridzuka Kofun 2 (Yamagata Prefecture). Few coffins survive intact due to decay, and many structural details remain unknown.The shape of the coffin's bottom is often preserved as an arc-shaped trace on the coffin bed, even when the coffin itself has decayed. == Timeframe and Scale == Waritakegata coffins appeared in the early Kofun period, sometimes even in late {{ill|Yayoi tumulus burials|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi tumulus burials|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}}. While originally placed inside pit-style stone chambers or stone cists, later periods saw coffins encased in clay or gravel ({{ill|clay coffins|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clay coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}}, gravel cists) or buried directly into grave pits. {{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobayashi Yukio|qq=}} established the connection between waritakegata coffins and burial facilities, and they are considered a defining feature of standardized early kofun burials.Morishta (2005), pp. 55–56. {{ill|Yoshirō Kondō|ja|近藤義郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshirō Kondō|qq=}} proposed that {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/keyhole-shaped tumuli|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|qq=}} served as ritual sites for the succession of chieftain spirits, highlighting: # A significant leap in mound shape and scale. # The presence of waritakegata coffins and pit-style stone chambers. # Grave goods, including numerous {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=}}, following specific norms.Kondō (1986). {{ill|Kazuo Hirose|ja|広瀬和雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazuo Hirose|qq=}} emphasized the unique features of early kofun, noting, for instance, the pairing of waritakegata coffins with pit-style stone cists in the {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurotsuka Kofun|qq=}} and {{ill|Moto Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto Inari Kofun|qq=}}, compared to {{ill|Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster|lt=Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster|lt=Akatuka Kofun|ja|赤塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akatuka Kofun|qq=}} in Oita, which used box-shaped stone coffins.Hirose (2003), pp. 155–156. The size of the burial chamber often correlated with the size of the coffin.Ōtsuka and Kobayashi, "Kofun Dictionary," 1982, pp. 367–368. Early examples measured 5–8 meters in length but became shorter over time, with late-period coffins averaging 2–3 meters. By the late Kofun period, boat-shaped coffins and large stone coffins gave way to box-shaped wooden coffins. == Notable Tombs and Kofuns == '''Yayoi period''' * {{ill|Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja|市三宅東遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichimiyake Higashi Site|qq=}} (Late Yayoi, Shiga Prefecture) * {{ill|Hirabaru Ruins|ja|平原遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirabaru Burial Site|qq=}} (Late Yayoi, Fukuoka Prefecture) '''Kofun period''' * {{ill|Eimoridzuka Kofun 2|ja|衛守塚古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eimoridzuka Kofun 2|qq=}} (Early, Yamagata Prefecture) * {{ill|Tōmimizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōmimizuka Kofun|qq=}} (Late Early, Miyagi Prefecture) * {{ill|Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurotsuka Kofun|qq=}} (Early, Nara Prefecture) == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Kofun Dictionary|last=Ōtsuka|coauthors=Kobayashi|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|year=1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|title=The Birth of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Kondō|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|year=1986|isbn=4-00-010266-4}} * {{Cite book|title=The State of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Hirose|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=2003|isbn=4-04-703355-3}} == External Links == * In addition to the waritakegata type, there were {{ill|boat-shaped wooden coffins|ja|舟形木棺|1=Funagata Mokkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/boat-shaped wooden coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Funagata Mokkan|qq=}}, named for their resemblance to boat prows. These are less commonly found. Most of the source wood for the waritakegata coffins, especially west of the {{ill|Kinki region|ja|近畿地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinki region|qq=}}, was evergreen conifers such as {{ill|Sciadopitys verticillata|lt=Kōyamaki|ja|コウヤマキ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sciadopitys verticillata|qq=}}, whereas regions east of {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi Province|qq=}} used {{ill|cedar|ja|スギ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cedar|qq=}}, {{ill|zelkova|ja|ケヤキ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zelkova|qq=}}, and {{ill|hinoki cypress|ja|ヒノキ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hinoki cypress|qq=}}.The {{ill|Kurozuka Kofun|lt=Kurozuka Kofun|lt=Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurotsuka Kofun|qq=}} contained a coffin made from {{ill|mulberry|ja|クワ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mulberry|qq=}} wood, suggesting the interred may have been connected to sericulture or possibly a female ruler.Examples include cedar from the Ichimiyake Higashi Site (Shiga Prefecture), hinoki from Hitachi Kagami Kofun (Ibaraki Prefecture), and zelkova from Eimoridzuka Kofun 2 (Yamagata Prefecture). Few coffins survive intact due to decay, and many structural details remain unknown.The shape of the coffin's bottom is often preserved as an arc-shaped trace on the coffin bed, even when the coffin itself has decayed. == Timeframe and Scale == Waritakegata coffins appeared in the early Kofun period, sometimes even in late {{ill|Yayoi tumulus burials|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi tumulus burials|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}}. While originally placed inside pit-style stone chambers or stone cists, later periods saw coffins encased in clay or gravel ({{ill|clay coffins|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clay coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}}, gravel cists) or buried directly into grave pits. {{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobayashi Yukio|qq=}} established the connection between waritakegata coffins and burial facilities, and they are considered a defining feature of standardized early kofun burials.Morishta (2005), pp. 55–56. {{ill|Yoshirō Kondō|ja|近藤義郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshirō Kondō|qq=}} proposed that {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/keyhole-shaped tumuli|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|qq=}} served as ritual sites for the succession of chieftain spirits, highlighting: # A significant leap in mound shape and scale. # The presence of waritakegata coffins and pit-style stone chambers. # Grave goods, including numerous {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=}}, following specific norms.Kondō (1986). {{ill|Kazuo Hirose|ja|広瀬和雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazuo Hirose|qq=}} emphasized the unique features of early kofun, noting, for instance, the pairing of waritakegata coffins with pit-style stone cists in the {{ill|Kurozuka Kofun|lt=Kurozuka Kofun|lt=Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurotsuka Kofun|qq=}} and {{ill|Moto Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto Inari Kofun|qq=}}, compared to {{ill|Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster|lt=Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster|lt=Akatuka Kofun|ja|赤塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akatuka Kofun|qq=}} in Oita, which used box-shaped stone coffins.Hirose (2003), pp. 155–156. The size of the burial chamber often correlated with the size of the coffin.Ōtsuka and Kobayashi, "Kofun Dictionary," 1982, pp. 367–368. Early examples measured 5–8 meters in length but became shorter over time, with late-period coffins averaging 2–3 meters. By the late Kofun period, boat-shaped coffins and large stone coffins gave way to box-shaped wooden coffins. == Notable Tombs and Kofuns == '''Yayoi period''' * {{ill|Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja|市三宅東遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichimiyake Higashi Site|qq=}} (Late Yayoi, Shiga Prefecture) * {{ill|Hirabaru Ruins|ja|平原遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirabaru Burial Site|qq=}} (Late Yayoi, Fukuoka Prefecture) '''Kofun period''' * {{ill|Eimoridzuka Kofun 2|ja|衛守塚古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eimoridzuka Kofun 2|qq=}} (Early, Yamagata Prefecture) * {{ill|Tōmimizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōmimizuka Kofun|qq=}} (Late Early, Miyagi Prefecture) * {{ill|Kurozuka Kofun|lt=Kurozuka Kofun|lt=Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurotsuka Kofun|qq=}} (Early, Nara Prefecture) == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Kofun Dictionary|last=Ōtsuka|coauthors=Kobayashi|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|year=1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|title=The Birth of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Kondō|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|year=1986|isbn=4-00-010266-4}} * {{Cite book|title=The State of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Hirose|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=2003|isbn=4-04-703355-3}} == External Links == * revision.comment: Bot: fix redirect [[Moto Inari Kofun]] → [[Motoinari Kofun]]
revision.text:
{{afc comment|1=Need to find a consistent nomenclature for all of these. Sekkan and Mokka need some level of consistency because of how they are contrasted here * [[Funagata Mokkan]] * [[Funagata Sekkan]] * [[House-shaped Sarcophagus]] * [[Box-shaped stone coffin]] * [[Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus]] * [[Sekkan]] * [[Waritakegata Mokkan]] * [[Waritakegata Sekkan]] * [[Wooden Coffin]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:43, 28 April 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Kuroduka kofun museum02s3872.jpg|200px|right|thumb|{{ill|Cinnabar|ja|辰砂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cinnabar|qq=}}-coated {{ill|Waritakegata wooden coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Waritakegata wooden coffin|13=User:Immanuelle/Waritakegata Mokkan|qq=}} replica inside the stone chamber of the {{ill|Kurozuka Kofun|lt=Kurozuka Kofun|lt=Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurotsuka Kofun|qq=}}. Visible are the base of the coffin and the {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirror|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirror|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=}}.]] '''Waritakegata Wooden Coffin''' (割竹形木棺, *Waritakegata Mokukan*) refers to a type of hollowed-out wooden coffin commonly found during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun period|qq=}}. == Overview == This type of coffin is made by splitting a log lengthwise, hollowing out its interior to create the coffin body and lid, and forming an elongated cylindrical shape. It was often placed inside a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=竪穴式石室|4=zh|5=豎穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pit-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}.
The waritakegata wooden coffin is believed to have been conceived as a solemn burial vessel for the chieftain class and privileged elites buried in ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun|qq=}}.
In well-preserved examples, the interior is divided into three sections: the deceased is placed in the central section, with grave goods arranged in the head and foot sections. The coffins were often placed on clay beds, gravel layers, or in chambers equipped with drainage systems, indicating meticulous construction of the burial facilities. Red pigments such as {{ill|Bengala|lt=red ochre|ja|ベンガラ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bengala|qq=}} (iron(III) oxide, Fe₂O₃) or cinnabar (mercury(II) sulfide, HgS) were sometimes applied to the interior and exterior of the coffin or the walls of the stone chamber. These acts are considered to have had ritual significance.Yoshitaka Hōjō, "Pit-style stone chambers and burial rituals," in {{ill|Makoto Sahara|ja|佐原真|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makoto Sahara|qq=}}, 2007, pp. 451–453. == Construction and Materials == The coffins are made by hollowing out giant trees 5 to 8 meters in length and approximately 1 meter in diameter, resulting in the "waritakegata" (split-bamboo) shape. Both ends of the cylindrical coffin were sealed with wooden boards.
In addition to the waritakegata type, there were {{ill|boat-shaped wooden coffins|ja|舟形木棺|1=Funagata Mokkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/boat-shaped wooden coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Funagata Mokkan|qq=}}, named for their resemblance to boat prows. These are less commonly found. Most of the source wood for the waritakegata coffins, especially west of the {{ill|Kinki region|ja|近畿地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinki region|qq=}}, was evergreen conifers such as {{ill|Sciadopitys verticillata|lt=Kōyamaki|ja|コウヤマキ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sciadopitys verticillata|qq=}}, whereas regions east of {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi Province|qq=}} used {{ill|cedar|ja|スギ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cedar|qq=}}, {{ill|zelkova|ja|ケヤキ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zelkova|qq=}}, and {{ill|hinoki cypress|ja|ヒノキ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hinoki cypress|qq=}}.The {{ill|Kurozuka Kofun|lt=Kurozuka Kofun|lt=Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurotsuka Kofun|qq=}} contained a coffin made from {{ill|mulberry|ja|クワ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mulberry|qq=}} wood, suggesting the interred may have been connected to sericulture or possibly a female ruler.Examples include cedar from the Ichimiyake Higashi Site (Shiga Prefecture), hinoki from Hitachi Kagami Kofun (Ibaraki Prefecture), and zelkova from Eimoridzuka Kofun 2 (Yamagata Prefecture). Few coffins survive intact due to decay, and many structural details remain unknown.The shape of the coffin's bottom is often preserved as an arc-shaped trace on the coffin bed, even when the coffin itself has decayed. == Timeframe and Scale == Waritakegata coffins appeared in the early Kofun period, sometimes even in late {{ill|Yayoi tumulus burials|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi tumulus burials|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}}. While originally placed inside pit-style stone chambers or stone cists, later periods saw coffins encased in clay or gravel ({{ill|clay coffins|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clay coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}}, gravel cists) or buried directly into grave pits. {{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobayashi Yukio|qq=}} established the connection between waritakegata coffins and burial facilities, and they are considered a defining feature of standardized early kofun burials.Morishta (2005), pp. 55–56. {{ill|Yoshirō Kondō|ja|近藤義郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshirō Kondō|qq=}} proposed that {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/keyhole-shaped tumuli|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|qq=}} served as ritual sites for the succession of chieftain spirits, highlighting: # A significant leap in mound shape and scale. # The presence of waritakegata coffins and pit-style stone chambers. # Grave goods, including numerous {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=}}, following specific norms.Kondō (1986). {{ill|Kazuo Hirose|ja|広瀬和雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazuo Hirose|qq=}} emphasized the unique features of early kofun, noting, for instance, the pairing of waritakegata coffins with pit-style stone cists in the {{ill|Kurozuka Kofun|lt=Kurozuka Kofun|lt=Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurotsuka Kofun|qq=}} and {{ill|Motoinari Kofun|lt=Motoinari Kofun|lt=Moto Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto Inari Kofun|qq=}}, compared to {{ill|Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster|lt=Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster|lt=Akatuka Kofun|ja|赤塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akatuka Kofun|qq=}} in Oita, which used box-shaped stone coffins.Hirose (2003), pp. 155–156. The size of the burial chamber often correlated with the size of the coffin.Ōtsuka and Kobayashi, "Kofun Dictionary," 1982, pp. 367–368. Early examples measured 5–8 meters in length but became shorter over time, with late-period coffins averaging 2–3 meters. By the late Kofun period, boat-shaped coffins and large stone coffins gave way to box-shaped wooden coffins. == Notable Tombs and Kofuns == '''Yayoi period''' * {{ill|Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja|市三宅東遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichimiyake Higashi Site|qq=}} (Late Yayoi, Shiga Prefecture) * {{ill|Hirabaru Ruins|ja|平原遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirabaru Burial Site|qq=}} (Late Yayoi, Fukuoka Prefecture) '''Kofun period''' * {{ill|Eimoridzuka Kofun 2|ja|衛守塚古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eimoridzuka Kofun 2|qq=}} (Early, Yamagata Prefecture) * {{ill|Tōmimizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōmimizuka Kofun|qq=}} (Late Early, Miyagi Prefecture) * {{ill|Kurozuka Kofun|lt=Kurozuka Kofun|lt=Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurotsuka Kofun|qq=}} (Early, Nara Prefecture) == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Kofun Dictionary|last=Ōtsuka|coauthors=Kobayashi|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|year=1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|title=The Birth of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Kondō|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|year=1986|isbn=4-00-010266-4}} * {{Cite book|title=The State of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Hirose|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=2003|isbn=4-04-703355-3}} == External Links == * These are less commonly found. Most of the source wood for the waritakegata coffins, especially west of the {{ill|Kinki region|ja|近畿地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinki region|qq=}}, was evergreen conifers such as {{ill|Sciadopitys verticillata|lt=Kōyamaki|ja|コウヤマキ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sciadopitys verticillata|qq=}}, whereas regions east of {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi Province|qq=}} used {{ill|cedar|ja|スギ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cedar|qq=}}, {{ill|zelkova|ja|ケヤキ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zelkova|qq=}}, and {{ill|hinoki cypress|ja|ヒノキ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hinoki cypress|qq=}}.The {{ill|Kurozuka Kofun|lt=Kurozuka Kofun|lt=Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurotsuka Kofun|qq=}} contained a coffin made from {{ill|mulberry|ja|クワ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mulberry|qq=}} wood, suggesting the interred may have been connected to sericulture or possibly a female ruler.Examples include cedar from the Ichimiyake Higashi Site (Shiga Prefecture), hinoki from Hitachi Kagami Kofun (Ibaraki Prefecture), and zelkova from Eimoridzuka Kofun 2 (Yamagata Prefecture). Few coffins survive intact due to decay, and many structural details remain unknown.The shape of the coffin's bottom is often preserved as an arc-shaped trace on the coffin bed, even when the coffin itself has decayed. == Timeframe and Scale == Waritakegata coffins appeared in the early Kofun period, sometimes even in late {{ill|Yayoi tumulus burials|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi tumulus burials|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}}. While originally placed inside pit-style stone chambers or stone cists, later periods saw coffins encased in clay or gravel ({{ill|clay coffins|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clay coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}}, gravel cists) or buried directly into grave pits. {{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobayashi Yukio|qq=}} established the connection between waritakegata coffins and burial facilities, and they are considered a defining feature of standardized early kofun burials.Morishta (2005), pp. 55–56. {{ill|Yoshirō Kondō|ja|近藤義郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshirō Kondō|qq=}} proposed that {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/keyhole-shaped tumuli|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|qq=}} served as ritual sites for the succession of chieftain spirits, highlighting: # A significant leap in mound shape and scale. # The presence of waritakegata coffins and pit-style stone chambers. # Grave goods, including numerous {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=}}, following specific norms.Kondō (1986). {{ill|Kazuo Hirose|ja|広瀬和雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazuo Hirose|qq=}} emphasized the unique features of early kofun, noting, for instance, the pairing of waritakegata coffins with pit-style stone cists in the {{ill|Kurozuka Kofun|lt=Kurozuka Kofun|lt=Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurotsuka Kofun|qq=}} and {{ill|Motoinari Kofun|lt=Motoinari Kofun|lt=Moto Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto Inari Kofun|qq=}}, compared to {{ill|Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster|lt=Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster|lt=Akatuka Kofun|ja|赤塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akatuka Kofun|qq=}} in Oita, which used box-shaped stone coffins.Hirose (2003), pp. 155–156. The size of the burial chamber often correlated with the size of the coffin.Ōtsuka and Kobayashi, "Kofun Dictionary," 1982, pp. 367–368. Early examples measured 5–8 meters in length but became shorter over time, with late-period coffins averaging 2–3 meters. By the late Kofun period, boat-shaped coffins and large stone coffins gave way to box-shaped wooden coffins. == Notable Tombs and Kofuns == '''Yayoi period''' * {{ill|Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja|市三宅東遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichimiyake Higashi Site|qq=}} (Late Yayoi, Shiga Prefecture) * {{ill|Hirabaru Ruins|ja|平原遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirabaru Burial Site|qq=}} (Late Yayoi, Fukuoka Prefecture) '''Kofun period''' * {{ill|Eimoridzuka Kofun 2|ja|衛守塚古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eimoridzuka Kofun 2|qq=}} (Early, Yamagata Prefecture) * {{ill|Tōmimizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōmimizuka Kofun|qq=}} (Late Early, Miyagi Prefecture) * {{ill|Kurozuka Kofun|lt=Kurozuka Kofun|lt=Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurotsuka Kofun|qq=}} (Early, Nara Prefecture) == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Kofun Dictionary|last=Ōtsuka|coauthors=Kobayashi|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|year=1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|title=The Birth of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Kondō|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|year=1986|isbn=4-00-010266-4}} * {{Cite book|title=The State of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Hirose|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=2003|isbn=4-04-703355-3}} == External Links == * {{afc comment|1=Need to find a consistent nomenclature for all of these. Sekkan and Mokka need some level of consistency because of how they are contrasted here * [[Funagata Mokkan]] * [[Funagata Sekkan]] * [[House-shaped Sarcophagus]] * [[Box-shaped stone coffin]] * [[Nagamochi style stone sarcophagus]] * [[Sekkan]] * [[Waritakegata Mokkan]] * [[Waritakegata Sekkan]] * [[Wooden Coffin]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:43, 28 April 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Kuroduka kofun museum02s3872.jpg|200px|right|thumb|{{ill|Cinnabar|ja|辰砂|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cinnabar|qq=}}-coated {{ill|Waritakegata wooden coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Waritakegata wooden coffin|13=User:Immanuelle/Waritakegata Mokkan|qq=}} replica inside the stone chamber of the {{ill|Kurozuka Kofun|lt=Kurozuka Kofun|lt=Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurotsuka Kofun|qq=}}. Visible are the base of the coffin and the {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirror|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirror|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=}}.]] '''Waritakegata Wooden Coffin''' (割竹形木棺, *Waritakegata Mokukan*) refers to a type of hollowed-out wooden coffin commonly found during the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun period|qq=}}. == Overview == This type of coffin is made by splitting a log lengthwise, hollowing out its interior to create the coffin body and lid, and forming an elongated cylindrical shape. It was often placed inside a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=竪穴式石室|4=zh|5=豎穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pit-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}.
The waritakegata wooden coffin is believed to have been conceived as a solemn burial vessel for the chieftain class and privileged elites buried in ancient {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun|qq=}}.
In well-preserved examples, the interior is divided into three sections: the deceased is placed in the central section, with grave goods arranged in the head and foot sections. The coffins were often placed on clay beds, gravel layers, or in chambers equipped with drainage systems, indicating meticulous construction of the burial facilities. Red pigments such as {{ill|Bengala|lt=red ochre|ja|ベンガラ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bengala|qq=}} (iron(III) oxide, Fe₂O₃) or cinnabar (mercury(II) sulfide, HgS) were sometimes applied to the interior and exterior of the coffin or the walls of the stone chamber. These acts are considered to have had ritual significance.Yoshitaka Hōjō, "Pit-style stone chambers and burial rituals," in {{ill|Makoto Sahara|ja|佐原真|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makoto Sahara|qq=}}, 2007, pp. 451–453. == Construction and Materials == The coffins are made by hollowing out giant trees 5 to 8 meters in length and approximately 1 meter in diameter, resulting in the "waritakegata" (split-bamboo) shape. Both ends of the cylindrical coffin were sealed with wooden boards.
In addition to the waritakegata type, there were {{ill|boat-shaped wooden coffins|ja|舟形木棺|1=Funagata Mokkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/boat-shaped wooden coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Funagata Mokkan|qq=}}, named for their resemblance to boat prows. These are less commonly found. Most of the source wood for the waritakegata coffins, especially west of the {{ill|Kinki region|ja|近畿地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinki region|qq=}}, was evergreen conifers such as {{ill|Sciadopitys verticillata|lt=Kōyamaki|ja|コウヤマキ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sciadopitys verticillata|qq=}}, whereas regions east of {{ill|Ōmi Province|ja|近江国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmi Province|qq=}} used {{ill|cedar|ja|スギ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cedar|qq=}}, {{ill|zelkova|ja|ケヤキ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/zelkova|qq=}}, and {{ill|hinoki cypress|ja|ヒノキ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hinoki cypress|qq=}}.The {{ill|Kurozuka Kofun|lt=Kurozuka Kofun|lt=Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurotsuka Kofun|qq=}} contained a coffin made from {{ill|mulberry|ja|クワ|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mulberry|qq=}} wood, suggesting the interred may have been connected to sericulture or possibly a female ruler.Examples include cedar from the Ichimiyake Higashi Site (Shiga Prefecture), hinoki from Hitachi Kagami Kofun (Ibaraki Prefecture), and zelkova from Eimoridzuka Kofun 2 (Yamagata Prefecture). Few coffins survive intact due to decay, and many structural details remain unknown.The shape of the coffin's bottom is often preserved as an arc-shaped trace on the coffin bed, even when the coffin itself has decayed. == Timeframe and Scale == Waritakegata coffins appeared in the early Kofun period, sometimes even in late {{ill|Yayoi tumulus burials|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi tumulus burials|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}}. While originally placed inside pit-style stone chambers or stone cists, later periods saw coffins encased in clay or gravel ({{ill|clay coffins|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clay coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}}, gravel cists) or buried directly into grave pits. {{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobayashi Yukio|qq=}} established the connection between waritakegata coffins and burial facilities, and they are considered a defining feature of standardized early kofun burials.Morishta (2005), pp. 55–56. {{ill|Yoshirō Kondō|ja|近藤義郎|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshirō Kondō|qq=}} proposed that {{ill|keyhole-shaped tumuli|ja|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/keyhole-shaped tumuli|13=User:Immanuelle/Zenpokoenfun|qq=}} served as ritual sites for the succession of chieftain spirits, highlighting: # A significant leap in mound shape and scale. # The presence of waritakegata coffins and pit-style stone chambers. # Grave goods, including numerous {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=}}, following specific norms.Kondō (1986). {{ill|Kazuo Hirose|ja|広瀬和雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazuo Hirose|qq=}} emphasized the unique features of early kofun, noting, for instance, the pairing of waritakegata coffins with pit-style stone cists in the {{ill|Kurozuka Kofun|lt=Kurozuka Kofun|lt=Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurotsuka Kofun|qq=}} and {{ill|Motoinari Kofun|lt=Motoinari Kofun|lt=Moto Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto Inari Kofun|qq=}}, compared to {{ill|Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster|lt=Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster|lt=Akatuka Kofun|ja|赤塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akatuka Kofun|qq=}} in Oita, which used box-shaped stone coffins.Hirose (2003), pp. 155–156. The size of the burial chamber often correlated with the size of the coffin.Ōtsuka and Kobayashi, "Kofun Dictionary," 1982, pp. 367–368. Early examples measured 5–8 meters in length but became shorter over time, with late-period coffins averaging 2–3 meters. By the late Kofun period, boat-shaped coffins and large stone coffins gave way to box-shaped wooden coffins. == Notable Tombs and Kofuns == '''Yayoi period''' * {{ill|Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja|市三宅東遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichimiyake Higashi Site|qq=}} (Late Yayoi, Shiga Prefecture) * {{ill|Hirabaru Ruins|ja|平原遺跡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirabaru Burial Site|qq=}} (Late Yayoi, Fukuoka Prefecture) '''Kofun period''' * {{ill|Eimoridzuka Kofun 2|ja|衛守塚古墳群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eimoridzuka Kofun 2|qq=}} (Early, Yamagata Prefecture) * {{ill|Tōmizuka Kofun|lt=Tōmizuka Kofun|lt=Tōmimizuka Kofun|ja|遠見塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōmimizuka Kofun|qq=}} (Late Early, Miyagi Prefecture) * {{ill|Kurozuka Kofun|lt=Kurozuka Kofun|lt=Kurotsuka Kofun|ja|黒塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurotsuka Kofun|qq=}} (Early, Nara Prefecture) == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Kofun
