revision.text:
[[ja:割竹形木棺]] [[ja:割竹形木棺]] [[ja:割竹形木棺]] [[File:Kuroduka kofun museum02s3872.jpg|200px|right|thumb|{{ill|Cinnabar|en|Cinnabar|WD=Q104614|lt=Cinnabar}}-coated {{ill|Waritakegata wooden coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Waritakegata wooden coffin|13=User:Immanuelle/Waritakegata Mokkan|qq=}} replica inside the stone chamber of the {{ill|Kurozuka Kofun|en|Kurozuka Kofun|WD=Q1793171|lt=Kurotsuka Kofun}}. Visible are the base of the coffin and the {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirror|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirror|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=}}.]] '''Waritakegata Wooden Coffin''' (割竹形木棺, *Waritakegata Mokukan*) refers to a type of hollowed-out wooden coffin commonly found during the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period}}. == Overview == This type of coffin is made by splitting a log lengthwise, hollowing out its interior to create the coffin body and lid, and forming an elongated cylindrical shape. It was often placed inside a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=竪穴式石室|4=zh|5=豎穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pit-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}.
The waritakegata wooden coffin is believed to have been conceived as a solemn burial vessel for the chieftain class and privileged elites buried in ancient {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=Kofun}}.
In well-preserved examples, the interior is divided into three sections: the deceased is placed in the central section, with grave goods arranged in the head and foot sections. The coffins were often placed on clay beds, gravel layers, or in chambers equipped with drainage systems, indicating meticulous construction of the burial facilities. Red pigments such as {{ill|Bengala|lt=red ochre|ja|ベンガラ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bengala|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁柄}} (iron(III) oxide, Fe₂O₃) or cinnabar (mercury(II) sulfide, HgS) were sometimes applied to the interior and exterior of the coffin or the walls of the stone chamber. These acts are considered to have had ritual significance.Yoshitaka Hōjō, "Pit-style stone chambers and burial rituals," in {{ill|Makoto Sahara|ja|佐原真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makoto Sahara|1=Makoto Sahara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11383046}}, 2007, pp. 451–453. == Construction and Materials == The coffins are made by hollowing out giant trees 5 to 8 meters in length and approximately 1 meter in diameter, resulting in the "waritakegata" (split-bamboo) shape. Both ends of the cylindrical coffin were sealed with wooden boards.
In addition to the waritakegata type, there were {{ill|boat-shaped wooden coffins|ja|舟形木棺|1=Funagata Mokkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/boat-shaped wooden coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Funagata Mokkan|qq=}}, named for their resemblance to boat prows. These are less commonly found. Most of the source wood for the waritakegata coffins, especially west of the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kinki region}}, was evergreen conifers such as {{ill|Sciadopitys verticillata|en|Sciadopitys verticillata|WD=Q161648|lt=Kōyamaki}}, whereas regions east of {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province}} used {{ill|Cryptomeria|en|Cryptomeria|WD=Q147388|lt=cedar}}, {{ill|Zelkova serrata|en|Zelkova serrata|WD=Q920344|lt=zelkova}}, and {{ill|Chamaecyparis obtusa|en|Chamaecyparis obtusa|WD=Q917187|lt=hinoki cypress}}.The {{ill|Kurozuka Kofun|en|Kurozuka Kofun|WD=Q1793171|lt=Kurotsuka Kofun}} contained a coffin made from {{ill|Morus (plant)|en|Morus (plant)|WD=Q44789|lt=mulberry}} wood, suggesting the interred may have been connected to sericulture or possibly a female ruler.Examples include cedar from the Ichimiyake Higashi Site (Shiga Prefecture), hinoki from Hitachi Kagami Kofun (Ibaraki Prefecture), and zelkova from Eimoridzuka Kofun 2 (Yamagata Prefecture). Few coffins survive intact due to decay, and many structural details remain unknown.The shape of the coffin's bottom is often preserved as an arc-shaped trace on the coffin bed, even when the coffin itself has decayed. == Timeframe and Scale == Waritakegata coffins appeared in the early Kofun period, sometimes even in late {{ill|Yayoi tumulus burials|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi tumulus burials|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}}. While originally placed inside pit-style stone chambers or stone cists, later periods saw coffins encased in clay or gravel ({{ill|clay coffins|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clay coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}}, gravel cists) or buried directly into grave pits. {{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobayashi Yukio|qq=}} established the connection between waritakegata coffins and burial facilities, and they are considered a defining feature of standardized early kofun burials.Morishta (2005), pp. 55–56. {{ill|Yoshirō Kondō|ja|近藤義郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshirō Kondō|1=Yoshirō Kondō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11638900}} proposed that {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun}} served as ritual sites for the succession of chieftain spirits, highlighting: # A significant leap in mound shape and scale. # The presence of waritakegata coffins and pit-style stone chambers. # Grave goods, including numerous {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=}}, following specific norms.Kondō (1986). {{ill|Kazuo Hirose|ja|広瀬和雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazuo Hirose|1=Kazuo Hirose|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11485567}} emphasized the unique features of early kofun, noting, for instance, the pairing of waritakegata coffins with pit-style stone cists in the {{ill|Kurozuka Kofun|en|Kurozuka Kofun|WD=Q1793171|lt=Kurotsuka Kofun}} and {{ill|Motoinari Kofun|lt=Motoinari Kofun|lt=Moto Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto Inari Kofun|qq=}}, compared to {{ill|Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster|lt=Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster|lt=Akatuka Kofun|ja|赤塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akatuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 川部・高森古墳群}} in Oita, which used box-type Sarcophaguss.Hirose (2003), pp. 155–156. The size of the burial chamber often correlated with the size of the coffin.Ōtsuka and Kobayashi, "Kofun Dictionary," 1982, pp. 367–368. Early examples measured 5–8 meters in length but became shorter over time, with late-period coffins averaging 2–3 meters. By the late Kofun period, boat-shaped coffins and large Sarcophaguss gave way to box-shaped wooden coffins. == Notable Tombs and Kofuns == '''Yayoi period''' * {{ill|Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja|市三宅東遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Late Yayoi, Shiga Prefecture) * {{ill|Hirabaru Ruins|ja|平原遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirabaru Burial Site|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Late Yayoi, Fukuoka Prefecture) '''Kofun period''' * {{ill|Eimoridzuka Kofun 2|ja|衛守塚古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eimoridzuka Kofun 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Early, Yamagata Prefecture) * {{ill|Tōmizuka Kofun|en|Tōmizuka Kofun|WD=Q11642380|lt=Tōmimizuka Kofun}} (Late Early, Miyagi Prefecture) * {{ill|Kurozuka Kofun|en|Kurozuka Kofun|WD=Q1793171|lt=Kurotsuka Kofun}} (Early, Nara Prefecture) == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Kofun Dictionary|last=Ōtsuka|coauthors=Kobayashi|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|year=1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|title=The Birth of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Kondō|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|year=1986|isbn=4-00-010266-4}} * {{Cite book|title=The State of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Hirose|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=2003|isbn=4-04-703355-3}} == External Links == * [http://example.com Example external link] {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|割竹形木棺|version=103099329|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:割竹形木棺]] {{wikidata link|Q11398019}} [[Category:Archaeological features]] [[Category:Archaeological remains]] [[Category:Coffins]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Japanese archaeological materials]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun (general concept)]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

revision.text:
[[ja:割竹形木棺]] [[ja:割竹形木棺]] [[ja:割竹形木棺]] [[File:Kuroduka kofun museum02s3872.jpg|200px|right|thumb|{{ill|Cinnabar|en|Cinnabar|WD=Q104614|lt=Cinnabar}}-coated {{ill|Waritakegata wooden coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Waritakegata wooden coffin|13=User:Immanuelle/Waritakegata Mokkan|qq=}} replica inside the stone chamber of the {{ill|Kurozuka Kofun|en|Kurozuka Kofun|WD=Q1793171|lt=Kurotsuka Kofun}}. Visible are the base of the coffin and the {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirror|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirror|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=}}.]] '''Waritakegata Wooden Coffin''' (割竹形木棺, *Waritakegata Mokukan*) refers to a type of hollowed-out wooden coffin commonly found during the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period}}. == Overview == This type of coffin is made by splitting a log lengthwise, hollowing out its interior to create the coffin body and lid, and forming an elongated cylindrical shape. It was often placed inside a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=竪穴式石室|4=zh|5=豎穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pit-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}.
The waritakegata wooden coffin is believed to have been conceived as a solemn burial vessel for the chieftain class and privileged elites buried in ancient {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=Kofun}}.
In well-preserved examples, the interior is divided into three sections: the deceased is placed in the central section, with grave goods arranged in the head and foot sections. The coffins were often placed on clay beds, gravel layers, or in chambers equipped with drainage systems, indicating meticulous construction of the burial facilities. Red pigments such as {{ill|Bengala|lt=red ochre|ja|ベンガラ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bengala|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁柄}} (iron(III) oxide, Fe₂O₃) or cinnabar (mercury(II) sulfide, HgS) were sometimes applied to the interior and exterior of the coffin or the walls of the stone chamber. These acts are considered to have had ritual significance.Yoshitaka Hōjō, "Pit-style stone chambers and burial rituals," in {{ill|Makoto Sahara|ja|佐原真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makoto Sahara|1=Makoto Sahara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11383046}}, 2007, pp. 451–453. == Construction and Materials == The coffins are made by hollowing out giant trees 5 to 8 meters in length and approximately 1 meter in diameter, resulting in the "waritakegata" (split-bamboo) shape. Both ends of the cylindrical coffin were sealed with wooden boards.
In addition to the waritakegata type, there were {{ill|boat-shaped wooden coffins|ja|舟形木棺|1=Funagata Mokkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/boat-shaped wooden coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Funagata Mokkan|qq=}}, named for their resemblance to boat prows. These are less commonly found. Most of the source wood for the waritakegata coffins, especially west of the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kinki region}}, was evergreen conifers such as {{ill|Sciadopitys verticillata|en|Sciadopitys verticillata|WD=Q161648|lt=Kōyamaki}}, whereas regions east of {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province}} used {{ill|Cryptomeria|en|Cryptomeria|WD=Q147388|lt=cedar}}, {{ill|Zelkova serrata|en|Zelkova serrata|WD=Q920344|lt=zelkova}}, and {{ill|Chamaecyparis obtusa|en|Chamaecyparis obtusa|WD=Q917187|lt=hinoki cypress}}.The {{ill|Kurozuka Kofun|en|Kurozuka Kofun|WD=Q1793171|lt=Kurotsuka Kofun}} contained a coffin made from {{ill|Morus (plant)|en|Morus (plant)|WD=Q44789|lt=mulberry}} wood, suggesting the interred may have been connected to sericulture or possibly a female ruler.Examples include cedar from the Ichimiyake Higashi Site (Shiga Prefecture), hinoki from Hitachi Kagami Kofun (Ibaraki Prefecture), and zelkova from Eimoridzuka Kofun 2 (Yamagata Prefecture). Few coffins survive intact due to decay, and many structural details remain unknown.The shape of the coffin's bottom is often preserved as an arc-shaped trace on the coffin bed, even when the coffin itself has decayed. == Timeframe and Scale == Waritakegata coffins appeared in the early Kofun period, sometimes even in late {{ill|Yayoi tumulus burials|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi tumulus burials|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}}. While originally placed inside pit-style stone chambers or stone cists, later periods saw coffins encased in clay or gravel ({{ill|clay coffins|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clay coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}}, gravel cists) or buried directly into grave pits. {{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobayashi Yukio|qq=}} established the connection between waritakegata coffins and burial facilities, and they are considered a defining feature of standardized early kofun burials.Morishta (2005), pp. 55–56. {{ill|Yoshirō Kondō|ja|近藤義郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshirō Kondō|1=Yoshirō Kondō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11638900}} proposed that {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun}} served as ritual sites for the succession of chieftain spirits, highlighting: # A significant leap in mound shape and scale. # The presence of waritakegata coffins and pit-style stone chambers. # Grave goods, including numerous {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=}}, following specific norms.Kondō (1986). {{ill|Kazuo Hirose|ja|広瀬和雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazuo Hirose|1=Kazuo Hirose|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11485567}} emphasized the unique features of early kofun, noting, for instance, the pairing of waritakegata coffins with pit-style stone cists in the {{ill|Kurozuka Kofun|en|Kurozuka Kofun|WD=Q1793171|lt=Kurotsuka Kofun}} and {{ill|Motoinari Kofun|lt=Motoinari Kofun|lt=Moto Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto Inari Kofun|qq=}}, compared to {{ill|Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster|lt=Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster|lt=Akatuka Kofun|ja|赤塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akatuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 川部・高森古墳群}} in Oita, which used box-type Sarcophaguss.Hirose (2003), pp. 155–156. The size of the burial chamber often correlated with the size of the coffin.Ōtsuka and Kobayashi, "Kofun Dictionary," 1982, pp. 367–368. Early examples measured 5–8 meters in length but became shorter over time, with late-period coffins averaging 2–3 meters. By the late Kofun period, boat-shaped coffins and large Sarcophaguss gave way to box-shaped wooden coffins. == Notable Tombs and Kofuns == '''Yayoi period''' * {{ill|Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja|市三宅東遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Late Yayoi, Shiga Prefecture) * {{ill|Hirabaru Ruins|ja|平原遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirabaru Burial Site|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Late Yayoi, Fukuoka Prefecture) '''Kofun period''' * {{ill|Eimoridzuka Kofun 2|ja|衛守塚古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eimoridzuka Kofun 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Early, Yamagata Prefecture) * {{ill|Tōmizuka Kofun|en|Tōmizuka Kofun|WD=Q11642380|lt=Tōmimizuka Kofun}} (Late Early, Miyagi Prefecture) * {{ill|Kurozuka Kofun|en|Kurozuka Kofun|WD=Q1793171|lt=Kurotsuka Kofun}} (Early, Nara Prefecture) == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Kofun Dictionary|last=Ōtsuka|coauthors=Kobayashi|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|year=1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|title=The Birth of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Kondō|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|year=1986|isbn=4-00-010266-4}} * {{Cite book|title=The State of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Hirose|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=2003|isbn=4-04-703355-3}} == External Links == * [http://example.com Example external link] {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|割竹形木棺|version=103099329|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:割竹形木棺]] {{wikidata link|Q11398019}} [[Category:Archaeological features]] [[Category:Archaeological remains]] [[Category:Coffins]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Japanese archaeological materials]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun (general concept)]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:pages]]

The size of the burial chamber often correlated with the size of the coffin.Ōtsuka and Kobayashi, "Kofun Dictionary," 1982, pp. 367–368. Early examples measured 5–8 meters in length but became shorter over time, with late-period coffins averaging 2–3 meters. By the late Kofun period, boat-shaped coffins and large Sarcophaguss gave way to box-shaped wooden coffins. == Notable Tombs and Kofuns == '''Yayoi period''' * {{ill|Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja|市三宅東遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Late Yayoi, Shiga Prefecture) * {{ill|Hirabaru Ruins|ja|平原遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirabaru Burial Site|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Late Yayoi, Fukuoka Prefecture) '''Kofun period''' * {{ill|Eimoridzuka Kofun 2|ja|衛守塚古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eimoridzuka Kofun 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Early, Yamagata Prefecture) * {{ill|Tōmizuka Kofun|en|Tōmizuka Kofun|WD=Q11642380|lt=Tōmimizuka Kofun|1=Tōmizuka Kofun}} (Late Early, Miyagi Prefecture) * {{ill|Kurozuka Kofun|en|Kurozuka Kofun|WD=Q1793171|lt=Kurotsuka Kofun|1=Kurozuka Kofun}} (Early, Nara Prefecture) == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Kofun Dictionary|last=Ōtsuka|coauthors=Kobayashi|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|year=1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|title=The Birth of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Kondō|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|year=1986|isbn=4-00-010266-4}} * {{Cite book|title=The State of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Hirose|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=2003|isbn=4-04-703355-3}} == External Links == * [http://example.com Example external link] {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|割竹形木棺|version=103099329|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:割竹形木棺]] {{wikidata link|Q11398019}} [[Category:Archaeological features]] [[Category:Archaeological remains]] [[Category:Coffins]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Japanese archaeological materials]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun (general concept)]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:pages]]

[[ja:割竹形木棺]] [[ja:割竹形木棺]] [[ja:割竹形木棺]] [[File:Kuroduka kofun museum02s3872.jpg|200px|right|thumb|{{ill|Cinnabar|en|Cinnabar|WD=Q104614|lt=Cinnabar|1=Cinnabar}}-coated {{ill|Waritakegata wooden coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Waritakegata wooden coffin|13=User:Immanuelle/Waritakegata Mokkan|qq=}} replica inside the stone chamber of the {{ill|Kurozuka Kofun|en|Kurozuka Kofun|WD=Q1793171|lt=Kurotsuka Kofun|1=Kurozuka Kofun}}. Visible are the base of the coffin and the {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirror|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirror|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=}}.]] '''Waritakegata Wooden Coffin''' (割竹形木棺, *Waritakegata Mokukan*) refers to a type of hollowed-out wooden coffin commonly found during the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}}. == Overview == This type of coffin is made by splitting a log lengthwise, hollowing out its interior to create the coffin body and lid, and forming an elongated cylindrical shape. It was often placed inside a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=竪穴式石室|4=zh|5=豎穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pit-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}.
The waritakegata wooden coffin is believed to have been conceived as a solemn burial vessel for the chieftain class and privileged elites buried in ancient {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=Kofun|1=Kofun}}.
In well-preserved examples, the interior is divided into three sections: the deceased is placed in the central section, with grave goods arranged in the head and foot sections. The coffins were often placed on clay beds, gravel layers, or in chambers equipped with drainage systems, indicating meticulous construction of the burial facilities. Red pigments such as {{ill|Bengala|lt=red ochre|ja|ベンガラ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bengala|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁柄}} (iron(III) oxide, Fe₂O₃) or cinnabar (mercury(II) sulfide, HgS) were sometimes applied to the interior and exterior of the coffin or the walls of the stone chamber. These acts are considered to have had ritual significance.Yoshitaka Hōjō, "Pit-style stone chambers and burial rituals," in {{ill|Makoto Sahara|ja|佐原真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makoto Sahara|1=Makoto Sahara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11383046}}, 2007, pp. 451–453. == Construction and Materials == The coffins are made by hollowing out giant trees 5 to 8 meters in length and approximately 1 meter in diameter, resulting in the "waritakegata" (split-bamboo) shape. Both ends of the cylindrical coffin were sealed with wooden boards.
In addition to the waritakegata type, there were {{ill|boat-shaped wooden coffins|ja|舟形木棺|1=Funagata Mokkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/boat-shaped wooden coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Funagata Mokkan|qq=}}, named for their resemblance to boat prows. These are less commonly found. Most of the source wood for the waritakegata coffins, especially west of the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kinki region|1=Kansai region}}, was evergreen conifers such as {{ill|Sciadopitys verticillata|en|Sciadopitys verticillata|WD=Q161648|lt=Kōyamaki|1=Sciadopitys verticillata}}, whereas regions east of {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province|1=Ōmi Province}} used {{ill|Cryptomeria|en|Cryptomeria|WD=Q147388|lt=cedar|1=Cryptomeria}}, {{ill|Zelkova serrata|en|Zelkova serrata|WD=Q920344|lt=zelkova|1=Zelkova serrata}}, and {{ill|Chamaecyparis obtusa|en|Chamaecyparis obtusa|WD=Q917187|lt=hinoki cypress|1=Chamaecyparis obtusa}}.The {{ill|Kurozuka Kofun|en|Kurozuka Kofun|WD=Q1793171|lt=Kurotsuka Kofun|1=Kurozuka Kofun}} contained a coffin made from {{ill|Morus (plant)|en|Morus (plant)|WD=Q44789|lt=mulberry|1=Morus (plant)}} wood, suggesting the interred may have been connected to sericulture or possibly a female ruler.Examples include cedar from the Ichimiyake Higashi Site (Shiga Prefecture), hinoki from Hitachi Kagami Kofun (Ibaraki Prefecture), and zelkova from Eimoridzuka Kofun 2 (Yamagata Prefecture). Few coffins survive intact due to decay, and many structural details remain unknown.The shape of the coffin's bottom is often preserved as an arc-shaped trace on the coffin bed, even when the coffin itself has decayed. == Timeframe and Scale == Waritakegata coffins appeared in the early Kofun period, sometimes even in late {{ill|Yayoi tumulus burials|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi tumulus burials|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}}. While originally placed inside pit-style stone chambers or stone cists, later periods saw coffins encased in clay or gravel ({{ill|clay coffins|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clay coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}}, gravel cists) or buried directly into grave pits. {{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobayashi Yukio|qq=}} established the connection between waritakegata coffins and burial facilities, and they are considered a defining feature of standardized early kofun burials.Morishta (2005), pp. 55–56. {{ill|Yoshirō Kondō|ja|近藤義郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshirō Kondō|1=Yoshirō Kondō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11638900}} proposed that {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} served as ritual sites for the succession of chieftain spirits, highlighting: # A significant leap in mound shape and scale. # The presence of waritakegata coffins and pit-style stone chambers. # Grave goods, including numerous {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=}}, following specific norms.Kondō (1986). {{ill|Kazuo Hirose|ja|広瀬和雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazuo Hirose|1=Kazuo Hirose|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11485567}} emphasized the unique features of early kofun, noting, for instance, the pairing of waritakegata coffins with pit-style stone cists in the {{ill|Kurozuka Kofun|en|Kurozuka Kofun|WD=Q1793171|lt=Kurotsuka Kofun|1=Kurozuka Kofun}} and {{ill|Motoinari Kofun|lt=Motoinari Kofun|lt=Moto Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto Inari Kofun|qq=}}, compared to {{ill|Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster|lt=Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster|lt=Akatuka Kofun|ja|赤塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akatuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 川部・高森古墳群}} in Oita, which used box-type Sarcophaguss.Hirose (2003), pp. 155–156. The size of the burial chamber often correlated with the size of the coffin.Ōtsuka and Kobayashi, "Kofun Dictionary," 1982, pp. 367–368. Early examples measured 5–8 meters in length but became shorter over time, with late-period coffins averaging 2–3 meters. By the late Kofun period, boat-shaped coffins and large Sarcophaguss gave way to box-shaped wooden coffins. == Notable Tombs and Kofuns == '''Yayoi period''' * {{ill|Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja|市三宅東遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Late Yayoi, Shiga Prefecture) * {{ill|Hirabaru Ruins|ja|平原遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirabaru Burial Site|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Late Yayoi, Fukuoka Prefecture) '''Kofun period''' * {{ill|Eimoridzuka Kofun 2|ja|衛守塚古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eimoridzuka Kofun 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Early, Yamagata Prefecture) * {{ill|Tōmizuka Kofun|en|Tōmizuka Kofun|WD=Q11642380|lt=Tōmimizuka Kofun|1=Tōmizuka Kofun}} (Late Early, Miyagi Prefecture) * {{ill|Kurozuka Kofun|en|Kurozuka Kofun|WD=Q1793171|lt=Kurotsuka Kofun|1=Kurozuka Kofun}} (Early, Nara Prefecture) == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Kofun Dictionary|last=Ōtsuka|coauthors=Kobayashi|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|year=1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|title=The Birth of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Kondō|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|year=1986|isbn=4-00-010266-4}} * {{Cite book|title=The State of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Hirose|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=2003|isbn=4-04-703355-3}} == External Links == * [http://example.com Example external link] {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|割竹形木棺|version=103099329|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:割竹形木棺]] {{wikidata link|Q11398019}} [[Category:Archaeological features]] [[Category:Archaeological remains]] [[Category:Coffins]] [[Category:Japanese archaeological materials]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun (general concept)]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:pages]]

These acts are considered to have had ritual significance.Yoshitaka Hōjō, "Pit-style stone chambers and burial rituals," in {{ill|Makoto Sahara|ja|佐原真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makoto Sahara|1=Makoto Sahara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11383046}}, 2007, pp. 451–453. == Construction and Materials == The coffins are made by hollowing out giant trees 5 to 8 meters in length and approximately 1 meter in diameter, resulting in the "waritakegata" (split-bamboo) shape. Both ends of the cylindrical coffin were sealed with wooden boards.
In addition to the waritakegata type, there were {{ill|boat-shaped wooden coffins|ja|舟形木棺|1=Funagata Mokkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/boat-shaped wooden coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Funagata Mokkan|qq=}}, named for their resemblance to boat prows. These are less commonly found. Most of the source wood for the waritakegata coffins, especially west of the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kinki region|1=Kansai region}}, was evergreen conifers such as {{ill|Sciadopitys verticillata|en|Sciadopitys verticillata|WD=Q161648|lt=Kōyamaki|1=Sciadopitys verticillata}}, whereas regions east of {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province|1=Ōmi Province}} used {{ill|Cryptomeria|en|Cryptomeria|WD=Q147388|lt=cedar|1=Cryptomeria}}, {{ill|Zelkova serrata|en|Zelkova serrata|WD=Q920344|lt=zelkova|1=Zelkova serrata}}, and {{ill|Chamaecyparis obtusa|en|Chamaecyparis obtusa|WD=Q917187|lt=hinoki cypress|1=Chamaecyparis obtusa}}.The {{ill|Kurozuka Kofun|en|Kurozuka Kofun|WD=Q1793171|lt=Kurotsuka Kofun|1=Kurozuka Kofun}} contained a coffin made from {{ill|Morus (plant)|en|Morus (plant)|WD=Q44789|lt=mulberry|1=Morus (plant)}} wood, suggesting the interred may have been connected to sericulture or possibly a female ruler.Examples include cedar from the Ichimiyake Higashi Site (Shiga Prefecture), hinoki from Hitachi Kagami Kofun (Ibaraki Prefecture), and zelkova from Eimoridzuka Kofun 2 (Yamagata Prefecture). Few coffins survive intact due to decay, and many structural details remain unknown.The shape of the coffin's bottom is often preserved as an arc-shaped trace on the coffin bed, even when the coffin itself has decayed. == Timeframe and Scale == Waritakegata coffins appeared in the early Kofun period, sometimes even in late {{ill|Yayoi tumulus burials|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi tumulus burials|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}}. While originally placed inside pit-style stone chambers or stone cists, later periods saw coffins encased in clay or gravel ({{ill|clay coffins|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clay coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}}, gravel cists) or buried directly into grave pits. {{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobayashi Yukio|qq=}} established the connection between waritakegata coffins and burial facilities, and they are considered a defining feature of standardized early kofun burials.Morishta (2005), pp. 55–56. {{ill|Yoshirō Kondō|ja|近藤義郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshirō Kondō|1=Yoshirō Kondō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11638900}} proposed that {{ill|Zenpōkōenfun|en|Zenpōkōenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpōkōenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} served as ritual sites for the succession of chieftain spirits, highlighting: # A significant leap in mound shape and scale. # The presence of waritakegata coffins and pit-style stone chambers. # Grave goods, including numerous {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=}}, following specific norms.Kondō (1986). {{ill|Kazuo Hirose|ja|広瀬和雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazuo Hirose|1=Kazuo Hirose|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11485567}} emphasized the unique features of early kofun, noting, for instance, the pairing of waritakegata coffins with pit-style stone cists in the {{ill|Kurozuka Kofun|en|Kurozuka Kofun|WD=Q1793171|lt=Kurotsuka Kofun|1=Kurozuka Kofun}} and {{ill|Motoinari Kofun|lt=Motoinari Kofun|lt=Moto Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto Inari Kofun|qq=}}, compared to {{ill|Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster|lt=Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster|lt=Akatuka Kofun|ja|赤塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akatuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 川部・高森古墳群}} in Oita, which used box-type Sarcophaguss.Hirose (2003), pp. 155–156. The size of the burial chamber often correlated with the size of the coffin.Ōtsuka and Kobayashi, "Kofun Dictionary," 1982, pp. 367–368. Early examples measured 5–8 meters in length but became shorter over time, with late-period coffins averaging 2–3 meters. By the late Kofun period, boat-shaped coffins and large Sarcophaguss gave way to box-shaped wooden coffins. == Notable Tombs and Kofuns == '''Yayoi period''' * {{ill|Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja|市三宅東遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Late Yayoi, Shiga Prefecture) * {{ill|Hirabaru Ruins|ja|平原遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirabaru Burial Site|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Late Yayoi, Fukuoka Prefecture) '''Kofun period''' * {{ill|Eimoridzuka Kofun 2|ja|衛守塚古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eimoridzuka Kofun 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Early, Yamagata Prefecture) * {{ill|Tōmizuka Kofun|en|Tōmizuka Kofun|WD=Q11642380|lt=Tōmimizuka Kofun|1=Tōmizuka Kofun}} (Late Early, Miyagi Prefecture) * {{ill|Kurozuka Kofun|en|Kurozuka Kofun|WD=Q1793171|lt=Kurotsuka Kofun|1=Kurozuka Kofun}} (Early, Nara Prefecture) == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Kofun Dictionary|last=Ōtsuka|coauthors=Kobayashi|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|year=1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|title=The Birth of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Kondō|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|year=1986|isbn=4-00-010266-4}} * {{Cite book|title=The State of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Hirose|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=2003|isbn=4-04-703355-3}} == External Links == * [http://example.com Example external link] {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|割竹形木棺|version=103099329|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:割竹形木棺]] {{wikidata link|Q11398019}} [[Category:Archaeological features]] [[Category:Archaeological remains]] [[Category:Coffins]] [[Category:Japanese archaeological materials]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun (general concept)]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

revision.comment: renaming these to the better title
revision.text:
[[ja:割竹形木棺]] [[ja:割竹形木棺]] [[ja:割竹形木棺]] [[File:Kuroduka kofun museum02s3872.jpg|200px|right|thumb|{{ill|Cinnabar|en|Cinnabar|WD=Q104614|lt=Cinnabar|1=Cinnabar}}-coated {{ill|Waritakegata wooden coffin|ja|割竹形木棺|1=Waritakegata Mokkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Waritakegata wooden coffin|13=User:Immanuelle/Waritakegata Mokkan|qq=}} replica inside the stone chamber of the {{ill|Kurozuka Kofun|en|Kurozuka Kofun|WD=Q1793171|lt=Kurotsuka Kofun|1=Kurozuka Kofun}}. Visible are the base of the coffin and the {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirror|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirror|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=}}.]] '''Waritakegata Wooden Coffin''' (割竹形木棺, *Waritakegata Mokukan*) refers to a type of hollowed-out wooden coffin commonly found during the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}}. == Overview == This type of coffin is made by splitting a log lengthwise, hollowing out its interior to create the coffin body and lid, and forming an elongated cylindrical shape. It was often placed inside a {{ill|pit-style stone chamber|ja|竪穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=竪穴式石室|4=zh|5=豎穴式石室|1=Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pit-style stone chamber|13=User:Immanuelle/Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}.
The waritakegata wooden coffin is believed to have been conceived as a solemn burial vessel for the chieftain class and privileged elites buried in ancient {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=Kofun|1=Kofun}}.
In well-preserved examples, the interior is divided into three sections: the deceased is placed in the central section, with grave goods arranged in the head and foot sections. The coffins were often placed on clay beds, gravel layers, or in chambers equipped with drainage systems, indicating meticulous construction of the burial facilities. Red pigments such as {{ill|Bengala|lt=red ochre|ja|ベンガラ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bengala|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 弁柄}} (iron(III) oxide, Fe₂O₃) or cinnabar (mercury(II) sulfide, HgS) were sometimes applied to the interior and exterior of the coffin or the walls of the stone chamber. These acts are considered to have had ritual significance.Yoshitaka Hōjō, "Pit-style stone chambers and burial rituals," in {{ill|Makoto Sahara|ja|佐原真|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makoto Sahara|1=Makoto Sahara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11383046}}, 2007, pp. 451–453. == Construction and Materials == The coffins are made by hollowing out giant trees 5 to 8 meters in length and approximately 1 meter in diameter, resulting in the "waritakegata" (split-bamboo) shape. Both ends of the cylindrical coffin were sealed with wooden boards.
In addition to the waritakegata type, there were {{ill|boat-shaped wooden coffins|ja|舟形木棺|1=Funagata Mokkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/boat-shaped wooden coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Funagata Mokkan|qq=}}, named for their resemblance to boat prows. These are less commonly found. Most of the source wood for the waritakegata coffins, especially west of the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kinki region|1=Kansai region}}, was evergreen conifers such as {{ill|Sciadopitys verticillata|en|Sciadopitys verticillata|WD=Q161648|lt=Kōyamaki|1=Sciadopitys verticillata}}, whereas regions east of {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|WD=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province|1=Ōmi Province}} used {{ill|Cryptomeria|en|Cryptomeria|WD=Q147388|lt=cedar|1=Cryptomeria}}, {{ill|Zelkova serrata|en|Zelkova serrata|WD=Q920344|lt=zelkova|1=Zelkova serrata}}, and {{ill|Chamaecyparis obtusa|en|Chamaecyparis obtusa|WD=Q917187|lt=hinoki cypress|1=Chamaecyparis obtusa}}.The {{ill|Kurozuka Kofun|en|Kurozuka Kofun|WD=Q1793171|lt=Kurotsuka Kofun|1=Kurozuka Kofun}} contained a coffin made from {{ill|Morus (plant)|en|Morus (plant)|WD=Q44789|lt=mulberry|1=Morus (plant)}} wood, suggesting the interred may have been connected to sericulture or possibly a female ruler.Examples include cedar from the Ichimiyake Higashi Site (Shiga Prefecture), hinoki from Hitachi Kagami Kofun (Ibaraki Prefecture), and zelkova from Eimoridzuka Kofun 2 (Yamagata Prefecture). Few coffins survive intact due to decay, and many structural details remain unknown.The shape of the coffin's bottom is often preserved as an arc-shaped trace on the coffin bed, even when the coffin itself has decayed. == Timeframe and Scale == Waritakegata coffins appeared in the early Kofun period, sometimes even in late {{ill|Yayoi tumulus burials|ja|弥生墳丘墓|1=Yayoi mounded tombs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi tumulus burials|13=User:Immanuelle/Yayoi mounded tombs|qq=}}. While originally placed inside pit-style stone chambers or stone cists, later periods saw coffins encased in clay or gravel ({{ill|clay coffins|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clay coffins|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}}, gravel cists) or buried directly into grave pits. {{ill|Kobayashi Yukio|ja|小林行雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kobayashi Yukio|qq=}} established the connection between waritakegata coffins and burial facilities, and they are considered a defining feature of standardized early kofun burials.Morishta (2005), pp. 55–56. {{ill|Yoshirō Kondō|ja|近藤義郎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshirō Kondō|1=Yoshirō Kondō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11638900}} proposed that {{ill|Zenpō-kōen-fun|en|Zenpō-kōen-fun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpō-kōen-fun|1=Zenpō-kōen-fun}} served as ritual sites for the succession of chieftain spirits, highlighting: # A significant leap in mound shape and scale. # The presence of waritakegata coffins and pit-style stone chambers. # Grave goods, including numerous {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=}}, following specific norms.Kondō (1986). {{ill|Kazuo Hirose|ja|広瀬和雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazuo Hirose|1=Kazuo Hirose|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11485567}} emphasized the unique features of early kofun, noting, for instance, the pairing of waritakegata coffins with pit-style stone cists in the {{ill|Kurozuka Kofun|en|Kurozuka Kofun|WD=Q1793171|lt=Kurotsuka Kofun|1=Kurozuka Kofun}} and {{ill|Motoinari Kofun|lt=Motoinari Kofun|lt=Moto Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto Inari Kofun|qq=}}, compared to {{ill|Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster|lt=Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster|lt=Akatuka Kofun|ja|赤塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akatuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 川部・高森古墳群}} in Oita, which used box-type Sarcophaguss.Hirose (2003), pp. 155–156. The size of the burial chamber often correlated with the size of the coffin.Ōtsuka and Kobayashi, "Kofun Dictionary," 1982, pp. 367–368. Early examples measured 5–8 meters in length but became shorter over time, with late-period coffins averaging 2–3 meters. By the late Kofun period, boat-shaped coffins and large Sarcophaguss gave way to box-shaped wooden coffins. == Notable Tombs and Kofuns == '''Yayoi period''' * {{ill|Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja|市三宅東遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Late Yayoi, Shiga Prefecture) * {{ill|Hirabaru Ruins|ja|平原遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirabaru Burial Site|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Late Yayoi, Fukuoka Prefecture) '''Kofun period''' * {{ill|Eimoridzuka Kofun 2|ja|衛守塚古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eimoridzuka Kofun 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Early, Yamagata Prefecture) * {{ill|Tōmizuka Kofun|en|Tōmizuka Kofun|WD=Q11642380|lt=Tōmimizuka Kofun|1=Tōmizuka Kofun}} (Late Early, Miyagi Prefecture) * {{ill|Kurozuka Kofun|en|Kurozuka Kofun|WD=Q1793171|lt=Kurotsuka Kofun|1=Kurozuka Kofun}} (Early, Nara Prefecture) == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Kofun Dictionary|last=Ōtsuka|coauthors=Kobayashi|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|year=1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|title=The Birth of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Kondō|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|year=1986|isbn=4-00-010266-4}} * {{Cite book|title=The State of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Hirose|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=2003|isbn=4-04-703355-3}} == External Links == * [http://example.com Example external link] {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|割竹形木棺|version=103099329|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:割竹形木棺]] {{wikidata link|Q11398019}} [[Category:Archaeological features]] [[Category:Archaeological remains]] [[Category:Coffins]] [[Category:Japanese archaeological materials]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun (general concept)]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

A significant leap in mound shape and scale. # The presence of waritakegata coffins and pit-style stone chambers. # Grave goods, including numerous {{ill|Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|ja|三角縁神獣鏡|1=Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sankakuen Shinjukyo mirrors|13=User:Immanuelle/Triangular-Rimmed Divine Beast Mirror|qq=}}, following specific norms.Kondō (1986). {{ill|Kazuo Hirose|ja|広瀬和雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kazuo Hirose|1=Kazuo Hirose|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11485567}} emphasized the unique features of early kofun, noting, for instance, the pairing of waritakegata coffins with pit-style stone cists in the {{ill|Kurozuka Kofun|en|Kurozuka Kofun|WD=Q1793171|lt=Kurotsuka Kofun|1=Kurozuka Kofun}} and {{ill|Motoinari Kofun|lt=Motoinari Kofun|lt=Moto Inari Kofun|ja|元稲荷古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moto Inari Kofun|qq=}}, compared to {{ill|Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster|lt=Kawabe-Takamori Kofun Cluster|lt=Akatuka Kofun|ja|赤塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akatuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 川部・高森古墳群}} in Oita, which used box-type Sarcophaguss.Hirose (2003), pp. 155–156. The size of the burial chamber often correlated with the size of the coffin.Ōtsuka and Kobayashi, "Kofun Dictionary," 1982, pp. 367–368. Early examples measured 5–8 meters in length but became shorter over time, with late-period coffins averaging 2–3 meters. By the late Kofun period, boat-shaped coffins and large Sarcophaguss gave way to box-shaped wooden coffins. == Notable Tombs and Kofuns == '''Yayoi period''' * {{ill|Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja|市三宅東遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichimiyake Higashi Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Late Yayoi, Shiga Prefecture) * {{ill|Hirabaru Ruins|ja|平原遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hirabaru Burial Site|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Late Yayoi, Fukuoka Prefecture) '''Kofun period''' * {{ill|Eimoridzuka Kofun 2|ja|衛守塚古墳群|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eimoridzuka Kofun 2|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Early, Yamagata Prefecture) * {{ill|Tōmizuka Kofun|en|Tōmizuka Kofun|WD=Q11642380|lt=Tōmimizuka Kofun|1=Tōmizuka Kofun}} (Late Early, Miyagi Prefecture) * {{ill|Kurozuka Kofun|en|Kurozuka Kofun|WD=Q1793171|lt=Kurotsuka Kofun|1=Kurozuka Kofun}} (Early, Nara Prefecture) == References == == Sources == * {{Cite book|title=Kofun Dictionary|last=Ōtsuka|coauthors=Kobayashi|publisher=Tokyo-do Shuppan|year=1982|isbn=4-490-10165-1}} * {{Cite book|title=The Birth of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Kondō|publisher=Iwanami Shoten|year=1986|isbn=4-00-010266-4}} * {{Cite book|title=The State of Keyhole-shaped Tumuli|last=Hirose|publisher=Kadokawa Shoten|year=2003|isbn=4-04-703355-3}} == External Links == * [http://example.com Example external link] {{Kofun navbar}} {{translated page|ja|割竹形木棺|version=103099329|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{wikidata link|Q11398019|ja|割竹形木棺}} [[Category:Archaeological features]] [[Category:Archaeological remains]] [[Category:Coffins]] [[Category:Japanese archaeological materials]] [[Category:Kofun]] [[Category:Kofun (general concept)]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]
